This game promises to take the frantic, unhinged gameplay of Vampire Survivors and bring it to an entirely new genre, which is a pretty tall order on its own. Thankfully, the developers managed to not only deliver on that promise, but may have gone even bigger than last time. While every location and playable character is taken straight from the original Survivors, they've all received new details that add some surprising depth to them that wasn't present before. The new gameplay has a pretty slow start, as you're a lot more restricted in terms of options than other card battlers; however, the game makes up for it with frequent unlocks and a level up system that gives out new cards like candy. What really sets this game apart is the combo system, where playing cards of ascending cost makes each subsequent card stronger. It's a system that gives you just that little bit of extra firepower you need in the early and midgame, and can be blown wide open once you have the right upgrades. Your build can go many different ways over the course of a run, your cards themselves can have a variety of modifications slapped onto them, and the huge cast of characters adds both a layer of strategy and a way to finetune your playstyle just the way you want it. It's a game that is designed to be broken, and there are of course secrets you can find if you, say, have a deck that can loop itself infinitely, for example. The game turns such ridiculous exploits into special challenges that reward you for finding and overcoming them, just like the original game.

My only real complaint for this game is that the difficulty curve can be somewhat temperamental at times. There are three transitional stages in the game that act as little more than walls that halt your progress and force you to grind for money to power up with. These stages are incredibly linear and extremely punishing, unlike the rest of the game which gives you far more freedom on how to approach things. The last one of these stages is the most egregious as there is only a single other stage in-between the previous linear stage and that one, and it forces a much more passive playstyle on you if you don't want to backtrack, or if you can't afford to unlock the one character who makes the money grinding process much easier like I couldn't on my first playthrough. I feel like there must've been a better way to scale the game's difficulty, but I can see this becoming less of a problem over time as new stages get added in future updates. The only other issue I had was some slight jank with keyboard controls, and the game completely locked up on me once at the end of a long session, forcing me to restart my entire PC as the game just wouldn't close even if I used Task Manager.

Overall though, I am a massive fan of this game just like its predecessor. It's got charm, a style that poncle can definitively call their own, and gameplay you're not gonna get anywhere else for at least the 6 months it'll take for the copycats to start coming out. I'm absolutely looking forward to seeing what the developers have in store for future updates, and any new crossovers that are on the way.

Basically, my opinion on this roguelike deckbuilder dungeon crawler with turn-based RPG combat is the same as when I reviewed the demo: “It’s alright.”

One thing I didn’t like about this hane is how heavily it recycles content from the previous game, to the point where all the characters and enemies are the exact same ones from Vampire Survivors, and there isn’t a single new design made specifically for this entry.

Another negative aspect is that the game is fairly short, with around 5 levels, each containing 2 or 3 sub-levels. The maps are also quite small and don’t offer much variety between them. The variety of weapons and upgrades is also pretty lacking compared to the previous game. Although, knowing the developer, it probably won’t take long before they start releasing free or paid updates to add more content.

Mechanically, the game is very simple, to the point where it’s relatively easy to create absurdly overpowered combos that let you destroy everything in your path.

Even so, it’s not a bad game if you enjoy this type of genre, especially if you like the Vampire Survivors aesthetic. And for 10$, you can’t complain too much.

[h1]Honestly... Wasn't Expecting This[/h1]
The Devs cooked with Vampire Survivors and now here they are coming out of the kitchen again with a new unique twist of their original game. I look forward to what they add in but for the time being it's definitely a  new twist on some old fashioned dungeon crawling! They kept everything from the original Vampire Survivors including characters, power ups and weapons and turned them into cards... Ok hear me out this really set a new bar for reimagining a original game. These guys really put their effort into this and it shows. The card system is easy to understand, There is a main hub you go to when you want to buy upgrades and when you want to add to the "Crawlers" team you assemble just a word to the wise though that you are limited on how many you bring with you so be smart about what you need or want Each character offers unique buffs that can help make the levels easier to handle. The upgrade system is unique this time around but you still get all the upgrades once you gathered the proper cards of course.
That being said I do recommend this game highly if you love the Vampire Survivors game or just a fan of good old fashioned Dungeon crawling! Either way it's well worth it and I look forward to what they add to the chaos.

[h1]Honest Trailers Parody:[/h1]

The trailer opens with the sound of a heavy, rusted gate creaking open, followed by a loud, wet [i]thud[/i]. A pixelated character appears on screen, standing perfectly still while a progress bar slowly crawls toward 100%.

[b]NARRATOR:[/b]
From the platform that heroically retired the Clown Award—only to replace it with a giant laughing emoji because Valve knows you can’t stop being toxic, you just needed a fresh coat of paint to do it...

Comes a digital experience that dares to ask the profound, philosophical question: "What if you took a spreadsheet, covered it in a retro filter, and convinced thousands of people it was a lifestyle—only for you to realize within forty-five minutes that you'd rather be doing literally anything else?"

[Title Card: VAMPIRE CRAWLERS]

[b]NARRATOR:[/b]
Look, it’s not all a digital desert. For the first five minutes, it’s a cozy, single-player sanctuary where you can finally escape from other human beings. It’s a low-pressure, turn-based card stacker that doesn't care if you're using a trainer to cheat your way to godhood. It’s the perfect game for people who want to feel productive without actually doing anything... for about half an hour.

[b]NARRATOR:[/b]
But once that initial hit of dopamine wears off, prepare for the most exhilarating experience you can have while technically being unconscious.

[h2]Pure Visual Noise[/h2]
Step into a world where the "art style" is just a tactical smokescreen designed to distract you from the absolute void of actual mechanics. Witness a collection of assets so old they qualify for a pension, heavily masked with so much shiny on-screen clutter it’s the digital equivalent of jingling neon keys in front of a toddler and calling it "High-Level Strategy."

Marvel as you walk in a very slow, continuous geometric circle, watching arbitrary numbers tick up while the game quite literally plays itself. It’s all the thrill of an epic fantasy adventure, perfectly captured in the format of a laundry cycle! Watch in awe as your hero battles for their life while you’re in the kitchen making a sandwich... and then realizing you’ve had more fun making the sandwich than playing the game.

[hr]

[b]NARRATOR:[/b]
But wait! The fun doesn't stop at watching a character walk. Prepare to navigate a deck overview and UI so spectacularly clunky, it feels like trying to perform bake a cake while wearing giant novelty foam fingers.

Experience "Strategic Depth" so remarkably shallow, it evaporates if you look at it too hard. Spend your limited free time on inventory management, hoarding digital trash like a pixelated dragon with a filing problem, all just to optimize your layout so your little sprite can walk in the exact same circle, but slightly more efficiently.

[b]NARRATOR:[/b]
Thrill at the illusion of choice! Will you pick the upgrade that gives you +2% damage, or the upgrade that gives you +2.1% damage? The tactical possibilities are literally finite!

And of course, who could forget [i]"Spicy Damage"[/i]? A mechanic that sounds like a fun, dynamic choice, but is actually just a confusing label for: "You are still just walking in a circle doing the exact same auto-attack, but now the font is red." Breathtaking. Please, hold your applause—you’ll need your energy to click "Next Turn" for the five-thousandth time.

[hr]

[b]NARRATOR:[/b]
But the true endgame doesn't start until you close the application and write a totally valid, negative review.

[h2]The Emoji-Only Apologists[/h2]
Watch in awe as a legion of players—who have spent the last three weeks perfectly content to watch a pixel auto-attack on a loop—suddenly discover a superhuman burst of energy the exact second someone dares to criticize their shiny object.

Observe the majestic, breathtaking irony as they mobilize to spam "Funny" awards, completely unable to process that a dissenting opinion exists. Marvel as they react with laughing faces to your nuanced critique, proving your point that typing an actual sentence requires more stamina than the game itself demands.

[b]NARRATOR: Starring...[/b]

    [b]The One-Hour Quitter[/b] (Who saw the void and walked away)

    [b]The Spreadsheet Spartan[/b] (Defending the geometric loop with their life)

    [b]The Laughing Face Goblins[/b] (Because the Jester died and they need a new hobby)

    [b]The Inventory Manager[/b] (I haven't seen a skeleton in three days, I'm busy sorting gems)

    [b]The Alt-Tab Architect[/b] (Building a better life in a different window while this plays itself)

    [b]And... The "Overwhelmingly Positive" Echo Chamber[/b]

[Bold Text on Screen: VAMPIRE CRAWLERS: THE COMMENT SECTION]

[b]NARRATOR:[/b]
Where the gameplay is entirely automated, but the passive-aggressive defense is strictly manual.

[b]NARRATOR (Whispering):[/b]
Vampire Crawlers: Because who needs gameplay when you can just watch a progress bar and pretend it's a hobby?

[b]NARRATOR:[/b]
Rated F for Functional enough to waste your money, but too hollow to respect your time.

[hr][hr][hr]

[h1]Ryan George Style Pitch Meeting:[/h1]

[b]Publisher:[/b] So, you have a first-person card-flinging simulator where we pretend to be a very fast person in a basement for me?

[b]Game Designer:[/b] Yes, sir, I do! It’s called Vampire Crawlers, and it’s a spin-off where we took the top-down perspective everyone found "functional" and threw it away for a view that makes you feel like you’re falling down a flight of stairs!

[b]Publisher:[/b] Oooooh, destroying the player's sense of direction is tight!

[b]Game Designer:[/b] It sure is! Now, in the last game, you could see the Mudmen coming from a mile away. But in this one, we put you in first-person so you have no idea why you're losing!

[b]Publisher:[/b] Wait, if I can't see the enemies, how am I supposed to use strategy?

[b]Game Designer:[/b] You don't! You just throw cards! It’s all about the "spectacle" of colors and lights while you move at Mach 5 through a hallway!

[b]Publisher:[/b] So it’s a tactical deck-builder for people who hate tactics and decks?

[b]Game Designer:[/b] Super easy, barely an inconvenience!

[b]Publisher:[/b] Oh my god. But isn't the actual gameplay a bit... well, shallow? It seems like it lacks polish, quality-of-life features, and people are quitting in under an hour.

[b]Game Designer:[/b] Whoops!

[b]Publisher:[/b] Whoopsie!

[b]Game Designer:[/b] Yeah, we totally forgot to make the game design deep or intuitive, but look at the art style! It’s so flashy that your brain won't have time to process how bored it is!

[b]Publisher:[/b] Oooooh, using vibrant presentation to hide a lack of substance is tight!

[b]Game Designer:[/b] Exactly! And the best part is the Steam community. Since Valve replaced the Clown Award with a Laughing Face, our fans can now mock critics with 100% more "positivity"!

[b]Publisher:[/b] Oh, I love those guys. How do they help?

[b]Game Designer:[/b] If anyone points out that the UI is a mess or that they hated it in forty minutes, the fans will show up to drop a 😂 emoji and call them "entitled" for wanting the game to actually be fun! How dare they!

[b]Publisher:[/b] Wow wow wow. Wow. So, it’s a disorienting, unpolished departure from a hit game, but it has a great presentation and a barrier of passive-aggressive emojis?

[b]Game Designer:[/b] That’s what we’re going with!

[b]Publisher:[/b] Well, okay then! I’m excited to put this on Steam and watch everyone wonder where the top-down camera went for the first forty-five minutes of their lives.

[b]Game Designer:[/b] Great! I’ll go tell Gennaro to start the marketing campaign!

[b]Publisher:[/b] It’s truly the perfect game for people who want to pay money to have a headache in a cool art style.

[b]Game Designer:[/b] It’s a $10 motion-blur delivery system that accidentally contains millions of skeletons!

The game is not BAD. It functions just fine and has some depth and fun to it. But it fundamentally misses the appeal of a deck-based game like its contemporary peers. There is no element of "What build will I find?" or stumbling into something cool. All the important decisions are made at the home base with what arcana and crawler(s) combo you'll use. After that, you run into the same suite of cards every time. You unlock more, but they all do the same things and there isn't nearly enough variety among the dozens of them to make any of the choices interesting. You are almost never making choices that matter, affect your strategy, or based on anything other than mana cost. You can do a lot worse for deck-building roguelites, but having this come out a month after STS2 isn't doing it any favors.

Vampire Crawlers plays like a classic first person dungeon crawler mixed with a modern roguelite. In a given run you'll select one (or later, more) characters to take into a dungeon and build a deck that gets ever more powerful over time.
It cleverly translates the core Vampire Survivors weapons and horde experience into a quick-flowing crawler. Balance, to me, felt a little off. Some weapons seemed much stronger than others, but as the source material is more focused on creating overpowerered builds that fill the screen with effects, and less on surviving on a razor's edge, it again feels fitting to the source material.
I played entirely on the Steam deck which handles the game well, though when I tried to build an infinite deck and played my two most special-effecty cards together immediately I did lose some frames. Generally it will run perfectly smooth, which is probably what you'd expect with a game like this.

The original Vampire Survivors revitalized the arena shooter genre with fresh blood and ideas, and more or less invented the Survival Shooter subgenre. The same dopamine-charged design principles have served Poncle well in the creation of this game, allowing them to hit on all the same strategic joys of deckbuilding and card management while retaining the bonkers numbers and incredible soundtrack their first game was known for. 

A fantastic addition to the roguelite deckbuilder genre, short and sweet at the time of writing and with plenty of flourish to go around. Poncle did it again, in so few words. 9/10.

Poncle has been nice enough to provide me with a code for this game (thanks!). A full written review will follow (albeit in Dutch), but I can't help but give this game some love here.

I've been given the code for pc but since I wholeheartedly believe in it I've gone out and bought myself a copy on PlayStation as well. Most of my gametime is on PlayStation and I've nearly got 100% in the game there. Soon I will.

Poncle is a master at taking simple game designs and spinning them out of control. This turn-based-card-slinging-dungeon-crawler is no different. It brings the same (or even more) charm that Survivors had. Loveable character homages who end up standing on their own. Now with voice acting! Progress is constant and you'll always be nudged to try out something new. Getting stronger and finding better ways to get your combo's of while dominating the dungeon feels like a delight.

What I've found is that Crawlers is a little easier than Survivors, so that initial Survivors starting bump that filtered some people isn't present here. Difficulty ramps up the further you get. You unlock more stages as you go but they're also a bit more bite sized. That said, the feeling of "one more" is still as present as ever.

In short, buy this game! You won't regret it. I'll go more in-debth in my written review once that comes out. It will be posted on: https://side-scroller.nl/.

I haven't played any good new game for a long time, but not this one, this one is so good.

So I'm going to push back on the crowd and suggest this game is not great. And that's because there is no build diversity and because the late game becomes a tedious slog.

After a certain point the only way to progress is endless combos. Endless combos are acheived by having enough wildcards, mana cards, and card draws. The other cards, the damage, armour, and damage modification cards, are just filler.

I'm trying to complete the last map and I play just a few encounters before I get bored. Here's how they go: 

On the deal I need to either quickly get a card draw or a boost of mana. There is a chance that that is impossible, but my deck is stuffed with those cards so it doesn't happen often. With that acheived, the combo begins. 5X. 10X Soon I have 30 cards in my hand (With no way to sort them! Arghh!) and more mana than I can use. I recycle the wildcards and mana cards and can keep it going until the card breakdown mechanic starts slowing it down. But usually the damage sponge enemies are dead by then.

The Crawlers abilities are helpful but not critical. The gems can be interesting but can't be part of a strategy because of how many cards you have and how many mostly useless gems there are. Mana, Draw. Wildcards. That's the game. And hunting through your hand to find a '2' mana card or a '3' mana card to keep the combo going. It's easy (but tedious) to progress. With all the systems in the game--the crawlers, the gems, the arcana--there is an illusion of depth to the game. But that's all it is: an illusion.

The games are also too long. It takes more than a couple hours to finish a run. Sure, there is a mid-game save feature, but a good rogue-like shouldn't take much more than a half hour for a run.

Anyways. The idea of the game is nice. The looks and sounds of the game are great. The beginning of the game is interesting, but it just doesn't scale well.

If you like Vampire Survivors and Slay the Spire, this game is a MUST BUY. It's like a combination of the best aspects from the two games.

My only "complaint", if you could call it that, is that it's a little short. Any other game, this would really worry me- but this is the Vampire Survivors crew. We can expect frequent additions by DLC and updates- many of them will shadow drop and add whole new elements to the game, just watch.

This scratches a very similar itch to Vampire Survivors--fast, flashy, fairly simple fun with somewhat limited depth. You can absolutely craft killer synergies (especially once you have multiple crawlers at once) and those will feel great. And the card combos match the first game, so returning players will enjoy that.

For new players it will require a little bit more effort to find and understand combos. The game doesn't do much to explain concepts like "luck" and "growth". But the sounds, lights, and retro fun should keep you engaged while you learn.

if you enjoyed the original Vampire Survivors, you’ll definitely enjoy this — and if you like card‑based roguelites, this is absolutely the game for you. Fans of the original will recognise many of the maps, characters, and items, but they’re brought back in new and interesting ways.

The gameplay feels a bit basic at first, but as you unlock more cards and mechanics it becomes far more engaging. The main premise is simple: clear levels, build your deck, and create powerful combos.

The game is just as addictive as the first one. Great experience overall, and I’d highly recommend it.

Incredible. The dopamine rush of achieving useless amounts of combo damage is like like a hard drug. I've hat moments where I went out of my way to do stupid amounts of overkill for no reason other than big number big sfx big rush.

People seem to find the ease with which the game can be broken unfun, and I understand that some may not enjoy exploring random bullsh*t go and seeing what can be done when RNGesus gives you lemons and expects you to kill a god. 
Normally, I am that kind of person, because death in most roguelites feels like time wasted - you didn't reach X point because RNGesus said no, and so you didn't get Y ressource, so now you have to try again and hope you succeed. Here, you get gold. Period. Every important unlock you get is by playing the different maps, without an arbitrary "Your Key is in another castle." and just for that I am already in love with the game. It's what kept me playing Vampire Survivors when it released - that feeling of no matter what I do, I get something for it, and if I had a goal, there is always a clear way to it. Somehow, poncle translated that to a deckbuilder.

Another biiiig plus is that I don't need to finish a run. I can just... leave, and return later. This means I won't get the burnout I felt from playing Vampire Survivors after however many hours I have in that game, where starting a run felt like a commitment, where even if I achieved what I wanted, I still felt chained until Death tore me away from the game.

I [b]hate[/b] Vampire Survivors and the consequences that its success inflicted upon game design, but Vampire Crawlers is actually pretty good and surprisingly restrained with all the flashy-jingly slot-machine nonsense. I mean, it's a Vampire _s game, so you can't avoid [b]some[/b] level of flashy-jingly slot-machine nonsense, but what can you do. It's pretty immediately compelling, though you start with just a little too much gated off. For me the, game only really started once I sank some decent points into maxing out how many cards I draw at the start of a turn, and then it only [b]really[/b] started once I unlocked legally-distinct-Bayonetta and hit my first infinite. And then it only [b]REALLY[/b] started when I [spoiler]killed that skelly-man jerk who tries to stop you from going infinite[/spoiler]. The combo system is simple enough to understand from a glance, but there's a lot of juice to wring out of it once you figure out what gems and cards let you functionally break it over your knee and casually throw out millions of points of damage in a single turn. I'm either going to wake up a few days from now [i]disgusted[/i] with this game as if it gave me a hangover, or the little one-quick-run shifts at the [i]Choose-from-Three-Things Factory[/i] are just going to keep adding up over a few months until suddenly I've piled up several hundred hours in it. I can't actually tell yet, but right now I'm leaning towards (and hoping for) enjoying it for some time longer.

A much more slow paced and accessible version of the vanilla content from Vampire Survivors, while also making some of the more useless items from that game actually worthwhile. If you ever felt stumped or bored by the bullet heaven gameplay loop of the first game, while acknowledging this will have not as much content, you'll have a fun time. Much less of a grind, visually easier to digest, and a rewarding gameplay loop that can become more complex at your leisure.

As far as issues go they're small, but still should be acknowledged. Selecting cards can be a pain when you have a lot in your hand as it's constrained to the small window on screen, which makes it hard to select specific cards when they're so scrunched together you can't even see what the card is from a glance. Some elements could stand to do away with RNG, like whether King Bible or Axes will actually proc knockback on bosses and it's especially frustrating when they're your method of avoiding damage in those fights. Runetracer is OP for clearing the growing rows of enemies in later levels, it pretty much trumps any other card in the game it's kind of absurd. The fact is does "RNG" damage is negligible when it's often a guaranteed mob encounter wipe on turn 1. Not to say it's inherently bad, but it does highlight how less effective other crowd control cards like King Bible, Fire Wand, Knives, and even the Santa Water often are.

Vampire Crawlers does away with all of that. After your first couple dungeons, the game presses hard on the gas and you start unlocking things and gaining power to the point it becomes parabolic. [i]And then you make the game harder on purpose so you can break it more.[/i]

Despite it being a turn based card game, this is not a slow, methodical game in the slightest, unless you're really thinking about your moves. It's very fast paced and it's crazy fun to watch the big numbers get exponentially bigger, just like the original Vampire Survivors (Which I most definitely have an embarrassing amount of playtime as well). That's not to say it's mindless, you are constantly deciding what cards go into your pile, and the decisions can make or break a run if you're not careful, and [i]it is so satisfying[/i] when you stumble into something that works and break the game. [i]And the game rewards you for it.[/i] It actively encourages you to do so.

I didn't think I liked Bullet Hells until Vampire Survivors, where it flipped the genre on its head and made [i]YOU[/i] the bullet hell. Funnily enough, I've dodged more ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ 'bullet patterns' in Vampire Survivors to where it made me like Shoot'em ups by proximity. I don't currently like Card game games, and yet here I am having the time of my life. I suspect I'll come to like even Slay the Spire someday.

4/5. There is a lot here to keep you playing for a good while, particularly in terms of unlocks and tinkering with different crawler combinations. Lots of flash and silly "number go up" moments as well. Plus the music is awesome.

As someone that prefers more complex games, and having played hundreds of hours of Slay the Spire 2 and Monster Train 2 before this came out though, I'm just not as gripped, hence the knock to 4/5. The build variety isn't as wide or deep (unless I need to get further into the meta progression). There are cool things like crawlers with different abilities and gems you can slot into cards. 

At the end of the day though, no matter which of the tons of weapon cards you pick or evos you get, the winning strategy is mostly the same: have enough draw, mana, and wilds to keep your combo going endlessly to raise your damage through the roof. If you want to fight the reaper(s), then add max health to that list.

It's still a great game, and like I said, there is a lot to unlock. If you're looking for interesting combos though, this doesn't have as much as I'd hope from a deckbuilder. And that's okay! Perhaps I'm just spoiled from the greats (STS2 and MT2 as I mentioned). This is still a great game for the price.

Fundamentally this is a good game. It's a rougelike deckbuilder with some very unique ideas. It's clear a core design philosophy was to capture the power fantasy vampire survivors can so often embody and it is very enjoyable to figure out combos that synergize well. 

That being said, the game is extremely breakable in a less than fun way. For example, I stumbled pretty quickly as soon as I unlocked the ability to use multiple characters on the combo of Poppea and Pugnala. Poppea's ability increases the duration of a character whenever a yellow card is played while Pugnala draws a card whenever a yellow card is played. I found these two characters together can very consistently and easily lead to semi-infinite loops. 

They're not quite infinite because the developers did clearly realize and tried to establish ways to negate infinites. Namely, whenever you use a card too many times in a turn it cracks and if you keep using it you risk breaking it and summoning a powerful enemy. But since cards uncrack if you simply end your turn as long as you're able to negate the enemy's attack which you basically always can, you can just rinse and repeat on the next turn. 

This becomes completely overpowered if you combine it with a card like Forever Heart which can give you more max health the higher your combo is. This led to an extremely boring round where I spent what felt like ages just looping to get my combo as high as I could until I had well over 1000 health at which point even the reaper at the end wasn't a huge threat. Bosses require a bit more strategy thanks to their mechanics allowing them to attack on your turn (especially true for the reaper, even with 1000 health while I could take a few hits I couldn't take many) but even then Clock Lancet trivializes that as long as you're careful to count your combo. I don't think infinites need to be impossible but the fact that I found myself reliably able to get into an infinite build within the first floor - it should ideally take a bit more friction. That being said you can always choose not to build an infinite, it's just a bit of a shame that it is so easy.

Aside from balance issues though the game is a pretty great time. It's certainly not going to be a massive challenge or anything, hit up Slay the Spire 2 if you want that, but if you want a fun power fantasy of a deckbuilder with some unique systems that are enjoyable to learn and break I can definitely recommend this game

Pros:
Fun deckbuilder with good depth.
Satisfying (and quite easy) to break the game.
If you like build optimisation, you will enjoy this game a lot.

Cons/areas for development:
Short, but fine for the price point (17h to complete main content).

There were a few hours I was so overpowered that it was starting to feel boring (but the last dungeon did have me close to death a couple of times).
Crawlers are not very well balanced, some are massively overpowered, others trash or redundant, so this does limit replayability for me.
Blue cards are (almost) worthless after the early game, some longer term utility would be nice for replayability.

They have done it again. Vampire Survivors was amazing, so is Vampire Crawlers. I can't recommend it enough. The only caveat is that there's still no cross saves between platforms, which seems like a must for this game.

The "Wild" part in the title is very fitting, because by the end of a good run you will have so many wildcards, that you can make nearly infinite combos. My highest was around 70 cards played in one turn.

I suggest you only use one crawler until you hit the last section, which is called "Cappella". It keeps up the challenge, without needing to grind. Overall it took me around 35 hours until I saw the credits for the first time and then another 5h to kill the optional bosses at least once and get all the achievements/unlocks.
I consider that a very good deal for 10€, especially because it was very addictive and fun throughout most of it, but by the end I was really "done" with it. I just didn't want to see any more cards. lol

That is mainly because the game however lacks a few VERY obvious QOL things, primarily being able to sort your card hand by mana usage or color. This not being implemented is an extreme oversight and once you have 40+ cards with hands just as large, it becomes a real chore to sift through the cards just to find the one you want. And you WILL have hands that large later on.

The other thing I'm personally missing are actual challenge runs, an infinite floors mode and a daily challenge.
These three seem like obvious features you want in a game like this, so I'm assuming they'll be added in later patches, seeing how Vampire Survivors also got a ton of new stuff over the years.

8/10

It's pretty shallow and bare bones with little variety. It's cheap but that doesn't really make it better.

It's fun up until you start getting access to reusable wild cards, then all legibility and any challenge goes out the window as you draw your entire deck and have to scroll through 20+ cards tightly packed together to continue your combo. Woe to anyone that attempts to play this with a keyboard and mouse, as picking out a specific card with a mouse at this point is nearly impossible. Yup, no hand size limit but also you only have a little control over your deck size, having a maximum of ten skips, twenty if you include banishing cards from showing up in the run. Methods of removing cards from your deck are few and far between outside of playing the same one multiple times on the same turn.

This makes many other mechanics redundant, like having to pay attention to how long you can keep your crawlers  active(the playable characters from vampire survivors, with their cards giving you a buff that triggers every time you play specific types of cards, ticking down in duration each time it triggers, with said card not returning to your deck until it wears off) or most of the unlockable arcana (an effect you can choose to have active for an entire run in the form of a tarot card).

For arcana, they're effects like "every 100 cards played, gain 1 max mana", "mana carries over between turns" and "crawlers return to the top of your deck when their effect expires". The first two become obsolete from meta progression (you can upgrade your starting max mana to just high enough that you really only need one or two mana generating cards to always have enough to play your entire hand/finish the combat in one turn). None of them do anything interesting like the ones from vampire survivors, and honestly it's kind of confusing considering how all in they went with infinite turns, etc.

It wouldn't be so bad if you simply could play without them, but late game throws damage numbers at you that requires being able to deal with encounters dealing 50 or more damage every turn so you can't really opt out of using all of the broken stuff handed to you.

Most of the gems you can slot into cards are redundant by the time you get wild cards, or pointless even before then. An example would be the Echo gem, which makes a card copy the last card played except it doesn't mention that it doesn't copy the effect of its gems or benefit from combo which is a vital mechanic for making cards remain relevant mid to late game stages (40 damage vs 200-400 for the lower end).

It also gets old waiting for all of your card effects to resolve at the end of combat, getting up for a glass of water and coming back to find they're still going. Yes overkilling something by an absurd amount can be funny. No it isn't as funny when you have to sit through nearly a minute of animations when you hit end turn to end the combat.

In short, its aesthetic is fantastic but mechanically the game is a mess. It wants to be something nuanced but also wanted to be a game where you can easily break it over your knee and see [b]BIG NUMBERS[/b], which pretty much chucks all of that nuance out the window, while also inflating the amount of time you spend in levels because of the lack of a maximum hand size (again, it is awful sorting through 20-30+ cards in your hand, all clumped together to find the right one to continue a combo).

This game basically has one gameplay mechanic: Play cards in ascending order of mana cost and the cards will combo.  That's it.  There's not a lot of depth here.  Combats basically play themselves, just combo with whatever you draw, and by default you only draw 3 cards so it's not like there's much choice anyway.  Every hand you draw is basically the same, which means every turn is the same, and every combat is just a repeat of the previous combat's turns.  It's very repetitive.  So far I've done like 4 or 5 runs and they have been quite easy and mindless.  I thought that unlocking more things would make the game more interesting, and it has slightly but not by much.  Deckbuilding is pretty unsatisfying -- the vast majority of the time, you're just going to be adding more copies of cards you already have in your deck, and you can't decline the card rewards.  

Overall I would say this game is quite a bit less fun than Vampire Survivors.  This has pretty shallow card gameplay mechanics and runs are very repetitive.  There is also a lot less visual charm I would say -- watching the animations of your weapons as they got stronger was pretty appealing in Vampire Survivors.  In this game it's 3D and first person and so you don't get a good overhead view of your attacks.  Your attacks don't fire off automatically (since you're playing them as cards) and so the game is a lot slower and less visually exciting.  The attack animations are not nearly as cool either.

I'm not sure if the game can be improved much either without fundamentally changing the core mechanics of the game, but maybe they can prove me wrong with some updates.  But yeah, I think a game like Slay the Spire 2 has so, sooo much more depth than this game -- it's not even close.  I am struggling to finish runs in this game and I've only played it for 4 hours.

I would say I got my money's worth in terms of the ratio of time:money spent, but that I'm disappointed at how easily "solved" of a deckbuilder this game was. I would give this game a middle rating if I could.

The first few hours are a great experience. Unfortunately, around the halfway point (pretty much when you unlock Pugnala), one particularly broken strategy for infinite card cycling becomes so easy to accomplish that the game basically becomes solitaire at that point.

It's not an exaggeration to say that I have been able to win virtually every fight on turn 1 before the enemy even gets a chance to act since discovering this strategy. In fights where I don't win on turn 1, or before the anti-infinite mechanics kick in, I've killed enough enemies that there is no frontline left to actually attack me when I end my turn. The AI winds up using its turns just to walk forward. Then I continue with the exact same gameplay on turn 2. At that point the game just becomes an exercise in spamming the same cards over and over again to juice your combo meter to absurd heights, and that's about it.

Unfortunately, that killed a lot of my interest in continuing to play.

BROKE: Add trigger limits to prevent infinites
WOKE: Summon a malignant deity upon going infinite

I used to have a boyfriend I actually spoke to. Now? We just sit in the same room, bathed in the neon glow of our monitors, silently vibrating as we farm XP and coins. We don’t talk. We don’t eat. We just crawl.

If you thought the original Vampire Survivors was a digital narcotic, this is the industrial-grade stuff they keep in the back of the vault. It’s like Slay the Spire if that game actually cared about your happiness and showered you with constant, unearned rewards instead of punishing your hubris for the 400th time.

Why you should (not) buy this:

- This game doesn't just give you a "win" every now and then; it opens a firehose of colorful pixels and "ding" sounds directly into your brain.
- My partner and I have reached a state of "Parallel Play" that borders on a monastic vow of silence. The only thing we share now is the electricity bill.
- You’ll say you’re done at 10:00 PM. Suddenly, it’s 4:00 AM, you’ve unlocked a secret character that’s just a sentient piece of garlic, and you’ve forgotten your own middle name.

In short: This game is a hazard to productivity, relationships, and basic human functioning. I highly recommend it. Now, if you’ll excuse me, I have another 30 minutes of a never ending overkill hand to play.  The reaper ain't got nothing on me.

Not as difficult as something like Balatro or Cloverpit, Vampire Crawlers lets you bang out balling combos without as much thought about synergies as the other games in this genre often require. While you can certainly employ such crazy synergies and combos here, so far I've not had to deploy too much theorycrafting in this game other than making sure I've got a variety of cards in the deck to draw cards, give mana, give knockback, armour, and deal damage etc - the core mechanic is the combo where you play a 0 mana card, then 1 mana, then 2 etc and they ramp up in power level from doing this. It's simple and satisfying - and as someone who struggled to finish Balatro and Cloverpit's hardest challenges because I am unable to intricately build my decks to be able to keep up with those challenges, here in Vampire Crawlers I'm able to enjoy all of the content and have a lot of fun.

The music is mostly (entirely?) remixes/rearrangements of the Vampire Survivors music but that's no bad thing as it's banging as ever (although strangely quiet by default).  The graphics are cool, I like the stylised 2D look in a smooth 3D world - reminds me of old style games. All your iconic pixel art from Survivors is here and looks great.

Most of the mechanics from Survivors are here too, including the very barebones first couple of playthroughs whiel you learn the ropes, and then it throws more and more mechanics at you over time, more items, etc. It's a lot of fun to rediscover it all. There's also a little more strategy required than Survivors - apart from a few challenge levels there most decent builds worked, whereas here you need to think a little bit about what you're building. I like the bridge levels here for a fun change of pace - they have super long waves of enemies which encourages you to build up in-battle stats like amount/damage etc which you can't normally do so effectively with the normal battles of just a few waves.

Performance is fantastic on Steam Deck, but also on an Android device via Game Native so I can play on the go. 

Another 10/10 from poncle. You get quite a lot of content for the price - I think I'll get all the achievements by hour 20 or so given where I'm at now.  So far the 100% has been quite enjoyable although one or two challenges have been a bit tedious.

Not quite as incredible a deal as Vampire Survivors but compared to a lot of games it's an incredibly fair price.

Welcome to Vampire Crawlers.  Your goal?  Make the most disgustingly overpowered combos possible to increase the power of your cards so you can annihilate the enemies.  It will not take you long to begin accomplishing this.  Synergies are a lot easier to grasp than they are in most other games like this, and once you unlock the right cards, gems, and crawlers, you'll effortlessly start gaining more than enough gold per run to begin unlocking things for future runs.  If you're doing things right, you can easily begin earning hundreds of thousands of gold per run, and the more gold you earn, the more easily you can stack the odds in your favor for future runs so you can earn more gold and style on your enemies even harder.

This game is also extremely friendly in regards to giving you control of the RNG as you move into the late game.  Don't like a particular gem?  You can seal it so it never appears again.  Feeling limited with the add-ons you can put on cards?  You can put multiple gem slots onto cards so you can stack multiple effects.

I won't call this game "easy", but I will say that once you understand and master the systems behind it, it becomes very straightforward and you can easily pull off combos that will shred even the final boss rapidly.  This game is a dopamine factory, and after you've beaten the game and earned all the achievements, they only thing you'll be left to ask is "When will the next DLC be?"  I've completed all the achievements, and I'll likely spend a few more hours on it just to make sure that when the DLC arrives, I'll be things set so I can annihilate it extra hard.

As with Vampire Survivors this game will cheerfully absorb hours and hours of your time. With familiar characters and combos it's easy for Survivors fanatics to get into, and is perhaps a little more accessible for those who found the concentration required for VS to be a little challenging. As with VS there isn't much obvious story but it's not that kind of game. Seeing the little sprites from VS up close and personal with a bit of time to appreciate the strategic complexities is undeniably charming. 

You start out underpowered and then quickly with a few unlocks grow in strength, there are some lovely quality of life enhancements like being able to skip whole floors for areas already completed which is handy if you're achievement hunting and just need to get to floor 4 of a large map. Although a little more stately in the opening hours the delightful chaos which is a pillar of Survivors is still there, appearing after a few hours whispering "I was there all along" in your ear as it sinks its teeth into your willing neck.

This game runs beautifully on PC, Mac and Deck, with ultrawide and dualsense support. Poncle are respectful of your time, and it's easy to quit, maintaining your progress on a run. 

In terms of value for money this will keep you entertained for hours and hours. It's a no brainer for those who enjoyed the first game, or who enjoy deck builders.

There is so much that can be said about this game, how enjoyable the gameplay is, the amazing sprite work and art design, how the team in my opinion genuinely managed to translate all the whimsy aspects from Vampire Survivors to this new genre, that I will leave the honest to god reviews to other people more capable for me.

As for my review, I’ll just write that Vampire Crawler is a gem of a game, it did really good taking the common dungeon crawling gameplay and adapted it perfectly to what I would have expected from a spin-off. Game is genuinely fun, engaging and if you want it, even really quick paced. For reasons, I recently started playing a new save through the smartphone version of Vampire Survivors and, it helped me realize that the jewel of the crown in the series no doubt will be the same for anything coming out of it: starting weak and slow and then something “clicks”, and enjoying the mayhem that comes afterwards when you discovers combos and tricks and become OP as hell while the screen becomes a bullet hell that would make ZUN proud. 

As for the bad things -and this comes with the dungeon crawler genre-, it’s very easily to misclick due the fast paced gameplay, and these tiny errors can cost you a run at higher difficulties. Vampire Crawlers fixes this by giving the player way more agency and tools to quickly become powerful, but still I know some people just don’t like when one of the core mechanics of a game is losing a ton while learning and earning new strats and items.

The game has a demo, so if you reading this one are curious about the game, give it a go, it has a decent size of content and even then, it’s nothing compared to the mountain that’s inside the actual game. Hopefully Vampire Crawler will receive later the same amount of updates and new content that Vampire Survivors got, but even if it doesn’t, the game is still really entertaining to play.

9/10

Excellent, well-paced, continually ramping up difficulty and cards and heroes and locations at a steady pace. They really executed what might at first seem like a crazy combination, but it totally works. Great on a gaming PC, SteamDeck, and works great on the MacBook Air M series (as does Slay the Spire 2 and Monster Train 2 - all excellent laptop games that pause while you work!).

A masterpiece in the Roguelite Deck Builder genre, if you aren't drawing your entire 40-card deck on your first turn in every encounter, you're not doing it right.

Some things I'd like to see:
Automatic hand sorting (drawing all those cards can make it difficult to decide what card to use next, perhaps it could be sorted by color to ascending mana cost order, putting cards at risk of shattering to the furthest left)
Endless/Inverse mode (Test how far you can take your deck against increasingly difficult enemies without fear of the Reaper in Endless, or go straight to the difficulty spike with Inverse if you find it too easy to overkill even basic enemies)
Drowner Reward (Even though it appears in the game, there is no reward for defeating the Drowner, maybe some sort of gem or relic that involves overflowing of some sort?)
Stalker and Maddener (Strangely, these two faux reapers are absent from the game, perhaps Stalker could be part of a mode where you need to navigate the map quickly, lest you get caught by it and have to fight it, I think that could be fun. As for the Maddener, it technically has a presence, but you never see it directly.)

I'm well aware this game is still in Early Access, so we may see these features sooner rather than later! That said, this still feels like a complete game in spite of that. And... Freaking.... Yoko Shimomura... I can't believe you got the legend herself to compose the title screen music for the game, if it weren't for everything else that makes the game amazing I would have given the game a 11/10 right then and there, but instead you'll have to settle for a 12/10.

I like this much better than Vampire Survivors and I liked that game quite a bit.   The card system is fairly simple, but very addictive.  You have crawlers which you recruit which add various cards and effects dependent on card colors.  There is a rather straight forward combo system.  Combos are directly correlated to mana card consumption so you will start with a 0 cost cards then stack to a 1 cost card to a 2 with each successive card causing more damage then if you were to play it solo.  Further, you will receive wild cards that will allow you to have out of sequence combos.  It's difficult to explain but it's fairly intuitive but very addictive.  One caveat I have with the system is that you can become overwhelmed with cards that facilitates a cluttered UI when in the heat of battle which may cause you to pick the wrong card messing up a combo.  Outside of that, this is an unpretentious, fun, compelling and cheap rogue like deck builder.  It has been a very pleasant surprise.  -LoA-

An excellent game that successfully transforms the core idea of Vampire Survivors into a card-based roguelite. In my opinion, the progression speed feels very well balanced, and the modifier system is thoughtfully designed, allowing players to fine-tune their playstyle in meaningful ways.

However, there is a balance issue in the later stages. Once the player assembles the combination of Mana Syphon and crawler characters like Poppea, Krochi, and Christine, the game effectively turns into an infinite combo simulator—provided the player gets enough wild cards and draw effects. This is particularly easy to achieve in Capella Magna and Ultima.

I also think some of the weaker Arcana—such as Swollen Fist and a few others—could use adjustments. Improving these would make initial Arcana choices more interesting, instead of relying mostly on picking them up later from Arcana chests. Perhaps weaker cards could gain additional draw effects or similar bonuses to increase their viability.

Additionally, the final bosses could benefit from more varied mechanics. Junk and Confusion were the only mechanics that occasionally disrupted gameplay in a meaningful way. Freeze and Mana Bomb only felt threatening when I wasn’t using Mana Syphon.

Overall, I strongly recommend buying this game.

[h1]It was by our hands that this series is once again given flesh[/h1]
Delivering the same level of addicting dopamine that made Vampire Survivors the smash hit that it was, this fun rogue-like combining dungeon crawling with deck building will make you want to be called here and wishing to pay tribute.

It's all in the cards - how you will fare in this game will mostly depend on what cards you decide to take with each level up. Each one boasts different effects when used, providing a surprising amount of depth. It was interesting to see how all the weapons and passives from VS were able to work as cards you use to fight enemies, along with some brand new ones. There's a bit of RNG involved and it can be frustrating when you're given bad hands, but fortunately this doesn't happen too often if you have a good deck.

The combo system allows for some insane plays once you understand how they work. Lower cost cards should be played first so they can then be followed up by the next cost up to increase the effects of the cards you use. It's not just what cards you play, but also the order you play them in - if you play your cards right, you can become insanely overpowered in no time. Cards that would otherwise see little value normally can become incredibly useful when played during high combos.
https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=3715086879
If you're looking for some hidden gems, this game has you covered! Finding gems along the way will allow you to upgrade your cards with special effects when played such as doubling or tripling damage dealt, creating coin cards, or my personal favourite, turning them into wild cards that always chains combos and costs 0 mana to use! These are usually found in chests and provide just as much dopamine upon opening as paid loot boxes - the main difference being you're not spending any money to open these.

Coins earned during runs can be used to upgrade powerups, giving you the helping hand you might need if you're struggling on a particular area. Cards can also be upgraded at the blacksmith for a fee, adding extra gem slots for that particular card in future runs so it's worth it to try and make as much gold as possible which is just as satisfying as ever to do, especially once you unlock the overkill relics later on.

Despite how addicting this game can get, runs can sometimes take a long time. While VS had up to 30 minute runs, dungeons here can take at least an hour to complete so it doesn't quite have the pick up and play experience VS had. Fortunately, you don't have to do an entire run in one sitting as you can save your progress mid-run by quitting to the title screen if you really need to take a break and return to it later.

This is also one of those games where it's actually worth completing all the achievements outside of showing off, as much like in VS, each one also unlocks in-game content such as new cards, gems and characters. You're actually being rewarded for your efforts, which is something I really wish more games nowadays did with achievements. That said, it only took me about 32 hours to complete everything, but I'm sure this game will get post-launch support that adds more content and unlocks.

Not only is this a great expansion of the Vampire Survivors series that converts the bullet-heaven formula into a fun deck builder, it's also a good gateway game to get into the genre while also having enough depth for more experienced players to enjoy. It's simple enough for newcomers to enjoy but not so simple that veterans of the genre will quickly find boring. If you're still thinking to yourself "I'm interested in this", don't wait for a sale - it's worth every cent! My words are certainly not empty when I say this, mankind really needs more games such as this.

But enough talk, have at this game!

If you thought Vampire Survivors was the ultimate productivity killer, poncle just doubled down. Vampire Crawlers takes that familiar, dopamine-soaked pixel aesthetic and shoves it into a fast-paced deck-building roguelite that feels like Slay the Spire on caffeine and holy water.

The Good
The Combo System: Unlike traditional deck-builders where you're constantly mathing out every turn, Crawlers encourages momentum. Playing cards in specific sequences (0-cost into 1-cost into 2-cost) creates explosive synergies that make you feel like a god of the dungeon.

Active Gameplay: Survivors was the king of "walking in circles," but here you’re actually pressing buttons. Choosing your path on the overworld and managing your mana adds a layer of strategy that keeps you engaged without losing that chaotic "screens-full-of-fireballs" charm.

Build Variety: Between the gems that add passive effects to your cards and the different Crawlers (characters) you can unlock, the theorycrafting is surprisingly deep for a game that costs less than a fancy sandwich.

Steam Deck Perfection: It runs like a dream. It’s the perfect "pick up for 15 minutes, accidentally play for 3 hours" game.

The Not-So-Good
100% is Quick: If you’re a completionist, you can probably max out the achievements in about 20 hours. It’s shorter than the original Survivors, but given the price and the "Early Days" feel, I expect plenty of free DLC updates.

Final Verdict
It’s addictive, it’s chaotic, and it’s dirt cheap. It doesn’t try to replace the original; it just gives you a new way to lose your sleep schedule to pixels. If you like roguelites even a little bit, this is an essential pick-up.

Better than Survivors, both in the core gameplay loop and in the fact that it knows when to end. Survivors is 100+ hours of content that gets stale about 15 hours in, while this is 30 hours of content that gets stale shortly before the 30 hour mark. It carries the same (temporary) addictiveness of its predecessor, but also the same problem of mind-numbing visuals and repetition. Just like Survivors, Crawlers is very, very, VERY easy to break, as the combo mechanic is incredibly easy to abuse. Whether its Crawler abilities that draw extra cards or Attractorb cards (I used both), its very easy to have your entire deck in front of you. Combine this with a consistent source of wild cards- I personally used Song of Mana/Mannaja and the Coin Card gem- and you'll be racking up insanely high combos that guarantee the enemy will never take a turn. While this is satisfying at first, it got old fast. Luckily, I unlocked the final achievement around the time I got tired of the gameplay, so I can at least say I had a good time the whole way through. Get it on sale, this isn't worth 10 bucks.

Very addictive and well designed. The gameplay is simple and easy to understand, but it’s amazing how naturally it scales and how the game guides you into discovering more items and mechanics.

The graphics and overall aesthetic are also incredible, full of character and originality, and the visual and sound effects work great together to tie everything together and make it feel really satisfying. I honestly didn’t even notice the hours going by lol.

The only downside I’ve found is that it’s way too easy to end up with “god runs” where you basically have infinite turns and armor and enemies can’t do anything about it. You don’t even have to try to force it, which honestly makes some runs feel a bit repetitive and boring towards the end.

Even though I feel like that’s a pretty big flaw, this game just leaves me wishing it was longer (honestly surprised it wasn’t) or had some kind of post-game content so I could keep playing.

In my opinin after the first 5 hours you've seen it all. Every run feels like a copy paste of the previous because the enemies don't really change their behavior at all which was fine for Vampire Survivors but for this game it doesn't quite work for me. Every run ends up feeling the exact same which I think is in part due to the combo system where you gain combo if you play cards in mana order (can be influenced in a few ways) which makes me look less at what cards do and more just how far you can push the combo because it makes each card more effective as you build more combo.

I think the game would be more interesting if there were different ways to build combos you could choose which would make you actually think more about what cards to pick for your deck beyond what mana cost they have. I think crawlers (characters) should also have a bit more variety in how they play one way or the other. At least to me it felt like some characters were OP because they have a card draw mechanic built into them and others just buff a stat up which felt less impactful early on. I know you can later on have multiple characters in the same run so probably that is where the scaling becomes better but it would still not make the gameplay feel different because ultimately the goal would still be to build the combo up and I already had a near infinite combo without trying.

I think building as high combo as possible is basically where all the fun of the game lies. If you want a very chill deckbuilder that doesn't require super much thought I think this is a great game. Just sit back, relax and turn brain off for the most part (just a little thought needed) but I think there's deck builders out there that are more interesting than this one.

I was a little bit worried, at first, when I saw in the trailer, that the game was now a deck-builder. 
But I can say with absolute certainty, that the vibes that made Vampire Survivors are not suffering from the genre change in the slightest. I am even willing to argue that the change was a stroke of genius and made the gameplay loop even more refined and focused. 
If you liked the picking-and-choosing of items and combinations in Vampire Survivors, then you will absolutely love Vampire Crawlers. 

Be aware: The game starts small, and I was afraid it might end up being shallow after the first couple of minutes, because there were only, like, 5 cards available. But the game opens up very quickly the more you play. After each dungeon, you can easily get something like 5-10 new unlocks and after just a few dungeons, the possibilities and available combinations absolutely explode. 

Eventually, you will run around with functionally unlimited mana, combos in the hundreds and game-breaking infinite(*) loops that put you into an absolute power trip!

I can rarely recommend a game as wholeheartedly as I can Vampire Crawlers.


*Make sure to not accidentally shatter your cards, or be prepared to face the consequences

The presentation and aesthetic of Vampire Crawlers is incredibly charming. The pixel art, 3D imaginings of Vampire-Survivors' maps, and sound effects all work extremely effectively in conjunction to form the basis of what the game is ultimately trying to achieve.

Some people have criticized the re-use of sprites from Vampire Survivors, but this isn't an issue I necessarily have with the game. On the other hand, after my hundreds of hours in Vampire Survivors, the return of these classic enemies was yet another thing I welcomed from this spin-off. Vampire Survivors spawned a genre for a reason- that entire game is flash after flash of brilliance, and hearing Poncle announce a new in-universe title understandably excited many people. Vampire Survivors carries more than enough on its bones to be worthy of establishing an IP.

I love Poncle, and Luca Galante's work has earned much of my respect over the years for the dedication he's shown not just to Vampire Survivors, but the 2020s indie-game scene as a whole. Rare is a developer who sells a classic game for the mere price of $5. Rarer is one who actually pulls off good crossover DLCs. They're cheap as hell, too. It's a beautiful trend in an ever-greedier industry, and I applaud them not just for their actions, but how they've influenced other studios to similar approaches.

And as much good as I have to say about Poncle, I regrettably cannot recommend this game.

The deckbuilding system is flashier than it is deep. The combo system is something, but in of itself does nothing more than vaguely ask for a minimum level of engagement from the player. There were very few times where I felt like the order in which I casted my cards actually mattered. Some cards, like the Attractorb, initially bought some excitement to my deck, but pretty quickly it turned into a pattern of cast, draw more, cast. Overall, the deckbuilding lacks genuine strategy, which sucks, because that's the minimum requirement for any good card game. It's almost like the devs know this, too- why else would there be a cast-all button? Clearly the deckbuilding wasn't intended for anyone who's ever played an actual card game. 

I'd be more okay with this mindless approach to deck building if the game embraced it in the way Vampire Survivors embraced its chaotic, power-scaling, nature, with builds quickly going exponential. Vampire Crawlers does not do this, and instead needlessly limits itself and therefore, your experience of it. A good example of this is the card-shattering system.

While the maps are charming on the surface, moving around them is actually pretty boring and there's almost nothing in terms of skill expression here. With the exception of finding occasional chests and food in some areas, it felt like how I moved around the map **really** didn't ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ matter which is extra frustrating given how much I really do like the style of them. The movement of Vampire Survivors presents far more mechanical depth than that of Crawlers- And that's about as basic as you get when it comes to movement in a 2D game.

Enemy Variety is nonexistent. Yes, different sprites have different HP and damage values but it never goes beyond that. Vampire survivors gets away with it, as it's an aspect of the genre it started, but this game doesn't. I'd hope for more interesting interactions between the enemies and cards, as any deckbuilder should do, but it's not there, yet.

The game isn't awful. The game attempts to be a dungeon crawler, deckbuilder, and roguelite, all at the same time, but it does none of these things particularly well. I think most of my gripes could be addressed relatively easily with a few patches, it doesn't seem like there's anything wrong with the core of what Poncle's going for. I wish the best for its success. I do not recommend it.

I'll join the small minority of negative reviews. I was a huge fan of the OG Vampire Survivors. I love deckbuilder roguelikes passionately. The review underneath might sound very negative, but I will preface everything by saying that the bones of Vampire Crawlers are good. The game has a lot of space to improve.

The game is fine. But that is it. Definitely the price point is not high, but the balance is very busted (this can change later of course) and the game turns into a slog extremely quickly.

I dislike the grindy meta-progression, I dislike the fact that (near?) infinite decks exist so easily, and that they require insane amounts of time to play, while not being particularly mentally challenging. I made an uncountable hundred card plays during my third run, and the game was essentially solved, but for maximal meta-progression I would have to play even more cards, completely pointlessly otherwise.

"Players will optimize the fun out of a game if given a chance" I definitely lived through that very early in the game, and I usually am a fan of optimization.

Some peeves: The combos cards create are not particularly engaging, and there is just too much busy work. Animations are slow and I didn't find options to speed them up enough. I hate that I have to play through hours of easy levels to get a challenge.

I definitely think there are the makings of a good game here. I can't recommend it, because in the end it boils down to my few runs feeling either shallow in gameplay depth or just boring. Most games that really make it in the roguelite deckbuilder genre have more engaging mechanics and require engaging the mind more actively. Right now the game is at an unthankful half way point between the casino experience of the original Vampire Survivor without the real time engagement, and the fun part of all time classics like Slay the Spire 2 and Monster Train 2. The middle between these two doesn't seem to work currently, maybe in the future.

Quite possibly the best casual Roguelite Deckbuilder currently available.

Vampire Crawler is based on the previous hit indie videogame vampire survivors which was focused on destroying massive hordes of enemies with a random selection of “choose 1 of 3” items to enhance your fighting capabilities against the creatures of the night. However, even though the genre has swapped from Action Roguelike to Deckbuilder, I have rarely ever seen a more faithful and fun adaptation in the gaming industry than Vampire Crawlers.

The game manages to take almost everything from the previous entry and somehow all the items, characters, mechanics, etc. still feel and play like their original counterparts.
This is extremely fascinating to me simply because it’s a complete genre shift, and they still accomplished this feeling of familiarity while being something entirely different gameplay wise.

I would usually start describing the gameplay by now, but Vampire Crawler is already a treat before the game even really starts. Once you get past the main menu screen, you will have to complete a basic tutorial on how the game works, and then you get send to the village which serves as your “hub area” for choosing upgrades, what characters to play and other useful sections. The Village is designed in such a fun way that it makes navigating the different parts very refreshing in comparison to other titles of its genre.

Before I go over to gameplay, I also want to mention my personal highlight of this game and that is its music. The soundtrack is incredible and fits the game’s theme and aesthetic perfectly. A super fun listening experience from start to finish that genuinely surprised me.

Now, let’s talk gameplay and there is a lot to cover here.
On paper, the concept is pretty simple. You get a set of cards based on the character you picked and then defeat rows and groups of enemies to gain experience points or gold. Most cards need mana to be played and once you’re out of mana, your turn ends and the enemies then get to attack or move forward. Once the enemy conducts their action, you draw a certain amount of cards and your mana replenishes again. This process repeats itself once the foe is vanquished or your life points have been reduced to zero (with no Tirajisú left) and you will be send back to the village. Just like most other Roguelite Deckbuilders, knowing which cards to pick and when to use them is crucial to be most efficient with mana usage, and Vampire Crawler is no stranger to that concept, they take the concept even further actually by adding a mechanic called “combos”. For example by playing a card that has a mana cost of 1, every card with mana value 2 in your hand gains a slight increase in its capability (more dmg, armor, etc.). After playing the 2 mana card, all 3 mana cards now get an increase to their stats. If you’re able to rack up the combo further and further upwards, these bonus stats can reach ridiculous amounts, but only if the chain of combos hasn’t been broken by a card with a lower, equal or too high mana cost value than the previous one. The amount of run customization in this game is actual insanity, since you can add on different abilities and effects on the cards and card-evolutions are brought back as well from the previous game, which is achieved by combining mostly 2 specific cards together into a high-cost nuke everything card.

During your run, you gain exp from slaying enemies and some other sources and that exp is then used to gain more cards from a selection of 3–4 random unlocked cards. Earned Gold will be brought back to the village to spend on new characters, status upgrades or giving cards more customization slots. The goal is to complete one floor after another to progress through the many different levels and reach the final boss of the level.

Runs can get crazy really fast, but the first few hours are relatively tame compared to mid- and especially late- game. This is where I am going to make my first and only mayor complaint about this game and that has to be its completion. All the achievements are extremely easy to obtain, and I finished it in around 20 hours with all achievements already under my belt. Now, for 10 bucks, it’s still 100% worth every penny since the time you spend playing it is incredibly fun and rewarding. However, if your trying to find a roguelite/roguelike to sink your teeth in and get challenged, this probably isn’t what you’re looking for.

In conclusion, Vampire Crawler is a perfect mix of its predecessor with the deckbuilder genre, accompanied by a great soundtrack and so many unique design and gameplay choices, which makes it one of the best released indie games of this year and definitely worth checking out.

And here I thought my Vampire survivors addiction was over...

This is a card game from Poncle so you can expect insane game breaking combos. Sadly, that's a double edged sword.

Because it's really fun when you realize how OP it is when you unlock and add the +2 crawlers to your party, equip the Chain link Arcana, and get your hands on some wildcards. The early gameplay is really fun. And my God the music department is still cooking!

But the problem is the same as it was with Vampire survivors, the lack of visual clarity and some huge lack of QoL features. 
This is not an issue in the early-mid game, but when you get to the endgame around Cappella Magna (or maybe even before), you start drawing your entire deck and it's a mess.
First of all, the game doesn't track your deck. Like, at all. There's a 'Deck pile' but that has a very different function and doesn't track mana, nor does it sort cards. 

But the worst is the way the game handles the Hand.
The End gameplay loop is something like this:
Fight an enemy, level up, get a card, Tab out to the notepad and update your own deck tracker. 
Enter bossfight. While fighting the trash you have an opportunity to buff yourself, because when the boss comes, you get hit after every 6th card. So what you will be doing is this: 
- Phase 1: Drawing your entire deck. Move all combat and single use buff cards to the right side ('locked cards'). The rest you arrange by mana, these will become your 'Pool'. Select from the 'Pool' a set of cards based on mana cost. -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6. one of each, draw, mana and buffs mixed for a rotation. I call these the 'Active cards'.
Start playing the 'Active cards' from lowest mana cost to highest, then a wildcard, repeat. Once you played a card a couple times, it cracks. If that happens you move the card to the 'locked' section of your hand. And replace the cracked ones with same cost cards.
When you draw new cards, they go to the left side of your hand. 
So your hand looks like this while playing: 'Active cards' | 'Pool' | 'Locked Cards' Now, I named and explained these sections because I will come back to them later. 
- Phase 2: When you have enough Might, Amount, Growth, Greed, etc you will switch to combat mode.
You do change your 'Active cards' from utility to combat cards and some mana or draw if needed. 
Oneshot the boss and get Ultra Mega Overkill. Good Times.

Now my problem with this is that there's absolutely no need for all the cards to be in one place. 
Lategame becomes a surgery simulator. Because you can't see nor select your cards easily/fast enough. It's gruelingly slow and frustrating (I spent from hours to up to half a day on single runs). Stressful too because selecting cards is quite janky and you will constantly be worried that you will missclick and ruin your 100+ combo. 
The UI doesn't help either. It's squished in the middle, with wasted free space on the sides, The HP indicator is covering the left side of the hand. And the cards are a bit too big, and they are way too crammed. 
'Active cards' the 'Pool' and 'Locked Cards' should be separated, but at least the 'Locked cards' should be able to be hidden. If not separating

And it needs a proper deck tracker. The current one only shows the full deck out of combat. In combat your cards are all over the place, Deck pile, discard pile, cards above the discard pile, Neither does track the cards by amount of mana, which is baffling, since the entire gameplay loop is about playing cards in ascending mana cost. I had to spend so much time to constantly update my own deck tracker in a notepad. 

These really ruined the fun of this game for me. By the end I was just rushing through the last couple achievements, and uninstalled it with relief. I finished it in almost 60 hours when it should've been 15-20. And the only reason it took this long is because of the lack of deck tracking, the janky card selection, and the horrible visual clarity.
I can recommend this game, but it desperately needs a couple of Quality of Life changes.

I've already played this enough (3-4 runs on the first map) to be able to give a postive recommendation. This is a fantastically crafted representation of Vampire Survivors in the deck builder genre. Only now its addictive properties are combined with systems that provide the player with a lot more agency and the satisfaction of tactical planning/execution. 

If deck builders appeal to you you'll definitely enjoy this. However, I'm not yet certain it ranks with other genre superstars (Slay the Spire series, Balatro) as many others are suggesting. There's no doubt a ton to do here (as VS before it, it's phenomenal value), and the first-person dungeon crawler perspective is a wonderful rendition of the Vampire Survivors world, but I'm not sure that it doesn't adhere too strictly to its conventions. E.g. you can't target enemies (though this provides for its own type of planning), the wave-based format of the fights doesn't seem necessary, and a lot of the incremental upgrades don't feel impactful. 

As well, the interface needs some refining in terms of delivering information to the player. E.g. how do I examine a character's traits outside of the inn? How can I decode what the active status yellow card effects are in battle after I've played them? They have icons and quantities, but no mouse-over text. It would be nice to have that aid until I memorize them. Lastly, the exit button's placement should be more obvious.
