Highly Recommend – 8/10

After spending 14 hours in the demo and another 24.5 hours in the full game, I can confidently give this an 8/10.

The game successfully captures what made Vampire Survivors great, but presents it in a completely different way. If you were turned off by Vampire Survivors' fast-paced gameplay but still liked the overall concept, this is a perfect alternative. The turn-based system lets you carefully plan each move, making every decision feel meaningful.

Like most roguelikes/roguelites, the early game can be tough, but it becomes more manageable as you progress. The game really opens up once you can run more than one crawler, this is where things get fun. The synergies can get absolutely wild, and you can create some of the most broken builds imaginable.

I'll admit the Milkmaid is probably overtuned and may get nerfed (at least at the time of review), but she's not required. All crawlers have strong builds, with or without her.

Game Mechanics

The weapon system from Vampire Survivors has been adapted into a card-based format. Just like in Vampire Survivors, weapons can be combined into stronger forms, and the combinations are the same here.

What this game adds is a slot system. Weapons can have slots where you insert gems that provide boosts, bonuses, and other perks. Later in the game, you can increase the number of slots, allowing some cards to hold two or even three gems, which adds another layer of strategy.

Pros:

Lets you play at your own pace; you can pause or quit mid-run and return anytime
Familiar weapon system with enough new mechanics to feel fresh
Turn-based gameplay makes each decision feel important and strategic

Cons:

Music isn't as polished as Vampire Survivors (though it may grow on me)
Some crawlers, weapons, and arcanes feel significantly stronger than others, balance could use some work
Hard caps on stats (e.g., +1000% EXP bonus) feel limiting and unnecessary

I'd like to think I'm just not the target audience and it's meant for a much more casual player, but, truthfully, I just can't see anyone finding this that much more entertaining than any of the other dozens and dozens of games just like this that have increasing difficulties allowing you to ease your way into thinknig hard, or just staying on low difficulties and doing the card game equivalent of button mashing.

[b]Vampire Crawlers[/b] is a great little vacation from deckbuilders that all walk the tight line of balance-obsession, opting to just go hog-wild. You’re not going to so much play a well-tuned game as much as you are going to enjoy breaking the hell out of it. I was skeptical with the demo being limited to what we could do with it, but after blazing through, 100%’ing all current content, I can safely say that this was a special treat, despite the lower difficulty and expectation from the player.

The core loop of Crawlers that keeps it engaging is learning to manipulate your combos to maximize your output. The early game will [i]feel[/i] like you aren’t getting a lot out of your synergies. A card draw here, some increased damage there. You’ll start off thinking that the bracer is the best card in the game, only to never use it again by the mid and late game as your card draw explodes out of control. Your 3 energy will go from “is this all I have?” to “I have 999 energy and can’t even end my turn because I keep cycling my 40 card deck forever.” Combo 4? How about combo 100. 5,000 gold to slot a card seem daunting? Try returning home with 2 million, one-shotting even Death by the end-game, and now you can just buy every upgrade and roflstomp as a stress relief after work.

To me, the game is a lot more engaging than Vampire Survivors, since you reach a point in VS where you just shut your brain completely off and let the game solve itself. I chased achievements in that game when it first came out, only to burn out on it that even the likes of Castlevania DLC couldn’t keep me glued, since the loop pretty much exhausted itself by the time I finished the base game content.

With Crawlers, I’m actually left itching for more, and am sad that I’ve now run out of content. It’ll likely be amusing to hop on and shatter it some more, but I want to savor the game like a fine wine, so I’ll gladly return to it when it gets its inevitable DLC with new cards to draft. I highly recommend the game if you like deckbuilding but want a more chilled, laid-back experience when you aren’t number-crunching in the more intense ones out there.

Day 1 thoughts:

Game leaves a strong impression, the gameplay loop is really fun and solid, poncle hit the mark again with the same magic that Vampire Survivors has

I love the 2D 3D perspective and moving around, the setting and atmosphere of the home town, it's something I wish Vampire Survivors had. The voice acting adds a TON of character and charm to existing characters. I didn't really care that much about some of them before, but I like them all now more than just from pure design/aesthetic, which was really the only thing I liked for most of the characters in VS.

Excited to see if the same kind of wild, cryptic secrets VS has are in this one once I clear more stages and unlock more characters and goodies.

Issues so far, which are subjective and something I need more perspective and experience to see if I feel the same about after, are that enemy health bloat starts feeling absurd from a beginner's perspective starting at difficulty 6, which might just mean I need to learn how to get better single-target dps in my decks for bosses (plus it's a roguelike where I'm meant to start weak and become much stronger through permanent upgrades between runs, so I'm waiting to form more of a verdict on this), and runs can take really long if I play more thoroughly (1h 30m+). Which is fine, it's just hard to set aside time for a run, compared to VS where I know my run limit will be between 15m to 30m, which makes it a convenient lunch break game. That's just the nature of a turn-based dungeon crawler game paired with my tendency to play games thoroughly, and I'll have to see how I feel about it long-term.

Excellent pickup for under 10 dollars, I can't wait to be off work after this review to play more.

As a final note, one particular Crawler's voice acting is so abysmally implemented that I immediately wanted to refund the game, only to find that I was too many hours in before I had unlocked it. Original review:
This is a difficult game to rate tbh.
At first it's a fantastic game, a fresh retake on the Vampire Survivor game that somehow manage to port the real time dopamine hit of the original game into a turn based card game.

The gameplay itself is pretty sleek and smart and I expect it to produce a couple of copy cats like Vampire Survivor did...

...and then you play a bit more of the game and realize that game is hironically enough way flatter than it's 2D counter part.

Unlike Survivor, Crawler is way more easily broken in ways that the game can't deal.
The game is extremely linear and offers little replayability, basically acting like a giant tutorial for an infite mode that doesn't exist, and once you reach the end of the game, you realize the only viable strategy is to push infinite combos where you use wildcards to reset combochain infinitely.

It wouldn't be that bad if unlike Suvivor, the gameplay of Crawler was zeny and allowed you to go with the flow, but instead the game becomes grindy and repetitive.

Once you finish the game then you are done. There isn't really any point in trying other builds because levels are fixed and repetitive and (as far as I know) the only extra challenge at the end is to kill the reaper which seem to be just a pretty annoying check on how many CC you've brough with you.

It's a bit sad really since Survivor itself was the perfect "infinite replayability and build diversity" game, while this one isn't at all.

I'll still give it a thumbs up because of how entertaining the game is for at least it's first half, and for it's very low price, but just know before you buy that a Survivor style game this isn't and expect to be completely done with it after 15h.

Mixed feelings for Vampire Crawlers. It's really quite impressive how much of the Vampire Survivors experience has been adapted into a card game, but at the same time it's really disappointing just how much it is pretty much just like Vampire Survivors. The colorful visuals, banging soundtrack and fun of chasing unlocks just like in VS is pretty much perfectly replicated, but as a card game the depth is severely lacking. After unlocking everything, I feel pretty much no itch to keep playing because there is very little strategy and variety in the deckbuilding aspects of the game. 

This is such a stark contrast to something like Slay the Spire 2 where the gameplay itself keeps you coming back long after you've unlocked everything. I think if you've never played any deckbuilding roguelike before, and you've never played Vampire Survivors, this is a good first intro to both of those things. For me, the experience quickly felt hollow and repetitive. Like Vampire Survivors, I'll look forward to revisiting after a bunch of new things get added so I can chase more unlocks, but until then, I find it hard to recommend.

I have so many things to say about this game, but I will try to keep this short.

First of all, if you're looking for a Slay the Spire level of depth, this game isn't it. It's very casual. Yet.
Second, this game is very well designed. You obviously start kind of weak, so you start mixing and tinkering your build until you find the sweet spot you've been looking for, only to find it's flawed in the next zone. This goes on and on until you realize you need more money to make it better to progress further. You also realize you didn't completely clear the early zones. So you go back and just wreck havoc, which is super satisfying because you spent the time building your optimal build. If you play MMOs you know what I'm talking about, because everybody tries to actively remove this aspect of their games.

There were some missing QOL features I wanted to mention, but I eventually figured out it's actually either a deliberate choice by the devs or an artifact you can find which unlocks the respective features. Which, again, goes back to how well designed this games is. I mean the things I didn't like turned out to be fixable by playing the game!

I guess my only gripe with the game is it does eventually become a bit easy once you figured out an optimal build as you collect new artifacts and power ups. But then again, you will hit this point after about 10, 15 or 20 hours of gameplay depending on how quick you are. And it's 10 dollars. Soooooo... Yeah. Again, very reasonable. There is also a way to circumvent certain restrictions, but I assume that's not intentional and will be fixed in the future.

This game has so much potential for future features. Maybe an enemy that hunts you through the map? An endless mode? Restricted build/rock-paper-scissors mode? Random encounters? I could even imagine a coop mode where you both have to progress through the map to unlock the next level. Some of these might as well be in the game but I just haven't found them yet, lol.

I really loved my time with vampire crawlers but I have a few major issues with it.

They mostly boil down to the enemies are very uninteractive and the ways to stop them are very limited. As such, a lot of builds end up feeling similar even if they are very different. I was very surprised there was no crit gem that lets non-crit weapons crit. I think evolutions also make the game worse because while huge damage upgrades, it feels like some are just different weapons. Scythe is the biggest problem here since Axe is very useful with knockback but Scythe just doesn't have that. Survivability is an issue as your combo is either so high you get 100+ armor instantly or you dont get enough to survive boss hits. 

I think an arcana that lets you summon gems as enemies would also really open up boss fights. because since boss are just 1 on 1, a lot of options like O'Sole's weapon and on kill effects become useless.

Overall I think the game needs a lot of work but is a very strong baseline! 

I am naming all of these problems because literally everything else about the game is so well put together its hard to accurately describe how satisfying, fast, and wild this game can be.

I mean it is good, and it has great game feel / responsiveness, but I don't feel like it has much skill expression. Maybe I haven't played for long enough (or maybe I'm just amazing at the game :P) but it feels like each hand you draw has one pretty obvious 'solution' for how to play it. This is especially true with the mana-stacking combo mechanic, which again feels great for player empowerment, but it makes each hand feel even more rigid and solved: obviously I'm going to play the cards I drew in order from lowest to highest pretty much all of the time.

I don't feel like I'm ever making very many meaningful choices in a dungeon crawl, unlike games like Slay the Spire where you are constantly weighing choices against each other. Sometimes you have to consider what gems to add to which cards, but even then it feels pretty simple.

And this imprecision is apparent in how the game is presented, too. Things are not very clear and deterministic for the player.
* It's not explained how any attack card chooses its targets (especially its splash damage) because for example what's the difference between how the fire wand chooses damage targets versus how the cross does so?
* You don't know how much health enemies have, so decisions like deciding to armor-up or to build up combo to go for the kill are just total guesses.
* You don't know how attacks like peachone or santa water are going to deal their extra damage in upcoming turns, so you can't really plan around it beyond guesstimating.

So I guess I'd recommend it for a power-trip kinda game, but not really as an actual strategic deckbuilder? I'll keep playing it and don't regret the purchase, but I would definitely go for other deckbuilders instead if I want an actual brain-on experience.

Having now completed the game 100% here are my thoughts:
Incredibly fun and addictive like Survivors was in early/mid game. Interesting card design, fun puzzle solving to try to maximise your combo and with that damage/armor output. OST and sound design in general is great. Playing high damage cards feels impactful with tons of projectiles being sent out. Boss design is also made interesting by allowing them to attack after you play a certain number of cards instead of just after you end your turn (and other things but no spoilers 😇).

BUT as soon as you get to later game the stages become way too reliant on you having some sort of an "infinite" scaling build or abusing certain crawlers to consistently one shot encounters. If you don't, you just die. These combos are incredibly easy to get by just grabbing some hand size, card draw and wildcards so progressing shouldn't be an issue. However, since getting these combos is so easy, they don't feel satisfying when you do manage to pull them off. 

These combos have another downside: clutter. You will be drawing your entire deck constantly making your hand incredibly cluttered and finding a specific card you want to play quite time consuming. Combining this with the fact that you'll need to play a LOT of cards each encounter to pull these combos off, each encounter drags out much longer than they need to.

If there was a neutral rating on steam, I would use that. Early and midgame are great but don't bother with late game unless you want to 100% this. Took me maybe 8 to 10 hours to get to the point where I had to start using "infinites" so consider if the price point is right for that playtime for you. Just hoping this receives balance changes for late game.

Poncle cooked hard here and then just gave up in the second half. 

I had to update my review to be negative because while it is an amazing dopamine filled experience at the start, it becomes incredibly easy, if not tedious at the end. Vampire Survivors did have that a bit but at least there are runs you need to do for unlocks where you are a bit weaker. The endgame is just filled with destroying everything turn 1 with quasi-infinite cycles without thinking much.

The game seems to be bugged at the moment or something because some cards never show up for me so I am unable to complete my collection in the upgrades section. There is no info in-game hinting at how you get them to appear but players say you apparently need to play their more basic versions for them to appear first. However, after multiple runs of cycling hundreds of the lower tiered versions to try to get something like Rainbow Armor, they have yet to appear and I have lost so much time for nothing. I saw some other players complain about not finding things so I suspect their is either a bug or there is some probability they need to fix.

Fights are just not really complex enough and you'll learn over time how much you might have been overthinking things. Red Death for example is insanely easy to beat with any freeze card in play if you understand how freezing and unfreezing works. And then you learn you shouldn't have even bothered with doing that because you can consistently one shot him once you are far enough in progression.

I would recommend playing the demo. All the good parts of the game lie there and it's free. I hope Poncle actually fixes issues with the endgame.

Vampire Crawlers is a pretty darn good transition of the formula that made Vampire Survivors so good into another genre.

It's a deckbuilding roguelite where you go around first person grid based dungeons leveling up and amassing cards that synergize with the character/characters you have equipped at the moment. You earn coins which you can use to get power-ups to make runs easier or potentially harder, unlock characters you've found, modify cards, etc. It's a relatively simple game compared to others in the genre, but that's kind of the point of the formula. You build big combos to do big damage and it makes all the pretty effects happen that make you addicted to it. 

I found it satisfying enough to play, but I wish there was just a bit more content. It's a proper price tag for the content you get, but I felt like it just ended before it got really good. Poncle has said they plan to do more content though, so yay! I should mention I put quite a few hours on the demo before this, so expect around 35 hours of content .

For achievement hunters, this game is relatively easy to get all of them. It's just grindy like how Vampire Survivors was, but fun grindy. 

It runs fantastically on my Steam Deck 512 GB LCD unit giving four hours of battery life with no adjustments of any kind needed. I do hope they make a setting to make the map a bit more bold or bigger in the future, as it's difficult to see if your eyesight ain't the best.

Very fun game that really captures the same feel Vampire Survivors does in terms of the power fantasy aspect. You're going to get strong QUICK, to the point that most runs pose little to no danger of actually failing. This in particular is a double edged sword for the game because while it is extremely fun to mow through hoardes of enemies after a certain point, most runs will eventually end up feeling the same.

However, before it reaches that point the game is an absolute blast to play through and through. The focus on a combo-based system makes playing through turns thoughtful as play order becomes crucial to strategy. The later stages even combat this combo-based system by having boss enemies punish you for combo-ing too hard with either damage, debuffs, or large shields. Additionally, there are systems in play to make sure you don't go true infinite as cards eventually break if you use them too much on the same turn. UNFORTUNATELY, this combo system gets easily exploited and eventually every deck you play is going to exploit the system in very similar ways. Mana, which was once a big limiting aspect of the game in the first few runs turns into something of an afterthought as you combo your way through having over 3000+ mana. Hand size, becomes a joke when you can combo your way through a handsize of over 50. Drawing cards is never an issue because a comboed out attractorb can draw your entire deck for 1 mana. Everything here is to draw out a VERY VERY ADDICTING power fantasy, but it also heavily removes any difficulty this game may have. 

As per Vampire Survivors, meta progression in Vampire Crawlers is also something very addicting to sink your teeth into, but also something that adds to the positive and negatives. Once you dig deep into the meta progression mechanics of the game, you can get strong QUICK. (There's a really OP combo I abused once you unlock having mutliple crawlers in one run utilizing Poppea and Pugnala that has insane ramp and allows you to basically go infinite after the first battle). Once again,  its FUN, but it also takes away from any difficulty this game may have. However, I will praise the meta progression as once you unlock a certain relic, you can basically customize every aspect of the game which can lead to extremely easy runs (Easier Wild cards) or extremely hard runs (No evolution, bad gem slots etc.)

As for the dungeon crawling aspect I actually quite enjoy it (probably more than most). It adds a bit of strategy in how you tackle each dungeon as fighting every single enemy in a floor will more often than not be the worst strategy. Until you unlock the ability to banish/skip cards on level up, filling your deck with random cards on level up can hinder your consistency. Which is why I appreciate that better routing is rewarded as you can have a very consistent deck while also making sure all important collectibles are still in reach. 

Anyways, I had ALOT of fun getting all the achievements in this game, but sadly I have no desire to return to it aside from getting more achievements (when they get added). With how easily it is to become OP each run eventually turned into doing the same thing over and over with no friction whatsoever. (strategy gets thrown out the window when you can draw your entire deck and have infinite mana lol). This unfortunately makes me a little sad because other strategies like slow control decks, become obsolete. Other rougelike deckbuilds do a good job at making other stategies viable and keeping the player in check. Whereas this game you're basically free to abuse it however you want

Which honestly, is probably what they were going for. This game hits the exact adrenaline rush and power fantasy Vampire Survivors used to give just in a rougelike-dungeon crawler-deckbuilder format, and delivers wonderfully. It's a great game but I would not say its a "forever game" like othe games in the genre like Slay the Spire. Eventually you'll run out of things to do, the dungeons arent ACTUALLY randomized so locales will stay the same but at the end of the day, once you hit those credits you probably had a blast doing so. 

This game is an easy recommend for someone who likes vampire survivors and likes the adrenaline rush of absolutely demolishing enemies. It won't replace games in the genre like slay the spire, and you probably wont be playing this game for THOUSANDS of hours but for its runtime, you'll get your 10(?) dollars worth easily. 

8/10

Vampire Survivors hooked me, so I was looking forward to the release of this game with some hope. I’m quite a big fan of card-based roguelikes myself, and after trying the demo, I realized the game had potential; it just needed further development.

Now, the full game has been released, and I am absolutely disappointed. I was expecting one of two things: either a fun, mindless time-sink (like VS) or a thoughtful card game based on combos (as is usually the case with this genre). 

In the end, this is what we got:
1) Mandatory comboing: You cannot play cards in the wrong order - and that is all you could get of the gameplay.
2) The card balance and effect information are terrible: it’s unclear why some cards with the same cost deal several times more damage than others.
3) No option to skip lvlup rewards: Your deck is forced to grow, and the cards you get are so random that maintaining some theme in it without a terrifying amount of luck is impossible.
4) Punishment for success: If you actually do get lucky and build a combo, don't celebrate yet - the game has two systems to punish you for it. First, you can only play the same card a limited number of times per turn. Second, every boss after the tutorial ones (from the demo) starts attacking you every N actions (e.g. last boss I fought attacked every my 3 actions, and could kill me in 4 hits).

Altogether, this caused such a strong feeling of rejection that I won’t touch this game ever again.

I was really excited to play this game, and I grabbed the demo as soon as it came out. The demo alone had me hooked.

While it has the Vampire Survivors look, it plays more like Slay the Spire lite. You've got a mana limit, hand size, block, all those familiar elements. But, much like Vampire Survivors, the gameplay loop instead becomes "how fast can I break the game?" Let me tell you, once you get going, not very long at all.

There is very little grinding that you need to do, often you'll hit a stage where you will struggle to get past the first couple of fights. Doing a run or two just to farm gold for upgrades will usually fix this and you'll be back to having infinite hands and steamrolling encounters. One small spoiler-lite tip: don't grind TOO much. There are tools that make it MUCH easier once you clear later stages that you'll find yourself overflowing near the end.

My one gripe is the blacksmith shop. There is currently no option to select multiple cards to upgrade, instead needing to select one by one. Having a ton of money to sink into card upgrades makes this tedious to go through. Hopefully the developers make a multi-select option for this later on.

Finally, this is one of the few games where I actively went to get all achievements. Most of them are basically just "play the game", but a few do require you to go out of your way. I thoroughly enjoyed my time, so far, and am very eager to see where future updates go.

I would strongly encourage anyone who is considering this game to grab the demo, it's a very good taste of what to expect in the full game.

It's the first turn.
I draw my 300th card, use my 600th mana and gain my 2000th Luck. All my other stats are maxed out.

I am a machine who turns cards into endless combos.
My cards used so many times they're all on the verge of shattering. 
After gaining my extra 1000th max health, I put the boss out of his misery by blasting him for 5000000x his HP bar. 

As with all runs, death approaces, but this time - I survive his first couple attacks. But my engine isn't quick enough to win the fight.


Jokes aside.
While I had a lot of fun with Crawlers, and would reccomend it for the first 15 hours, after awhile it does become trivial. Endless combos are too easy, and while I love "breaking" games, this has been too easy to break, and too broken when it is. At a certain point when the novelty wore off, managing my hand of 45 cards to optimize a combo, then draw my whole deck and repeat to make it higher, became a chore more than an enjoyment. When death came, I survived his hit, but even with my run lasting 5 hours I still hadn't made an engine that could come online fast enough to beat him. But the thing is, I could have, I could have spent every single encounter strictly doing a combo optimimally so that I could have had enough stats to survive the final fight. But then my run would have been 10 hours, with only the first hour being fun and the rest being a massive chore.
FYI, i'd reccomend atleast 2000hp, some good armor gain, or a lot of disarming. My deck had neither of the final 2. 
I might come back to it one day, but I can't be bothered doing a pointless 5 hour run for 1 fight again.

TLDR Needs more content and there is currently a game-breaking bug that makes it impossible to get all achievements and play the game normally. For people who are already VS fans, this game may be acceptable, but probably not for anyone else, and certainly not for serious fans of the deckbuilder genre.

Notice that most positive reviews have less than 20 hrs, ie, people who played this game in one 5-6 hr session, said to themselves, "OMG colors effects VAMPIRE STUFF" (note: the game has no vampires), and posted a positive review. And notice that none of them have played the game since either.

The problem is this game doesn't have anywhere near the amount of content and depth of Vampire Survivors while running at twice the price, which is not ever going to be a formula for success.

The strategy aspect is quite fun for the first 5 - 6 hours. However, the game allows you to completely break its own gameplay loop around 10 hrs in, which is really not smart. It's extremely easy to set up infinite combos and the game does very little to rein that in. Once you realize you can do it, there's no reason to play any other way. Of course, SOMEHOW there's no way to sort cards by cost in your hand, so this most effective way to play is MIND-NUMBINGLY BORING. Talk about a carrot and a stick.

This would be okay if the game addressed it squarely, which it doesn't. The game needs challenge modes to prevent combo abuse, or higher difficulties, or something. But it doesn't. The game lets you break its own gameplay loop, and then that's it. You're done. You win.

Why aren't there challenge modes? Why not put out challenge modes for specific teams with specific dungeons with specific item sets? Why aren't there daily seeds? There is so much opportunity for depth here and NONE OF IT IS BEING TAKEN.

Poncle has evolved far beyond a one-man company making web games, and they hired A WHOLE OTHER STUDIO to help them with this. The demo dropped 6 weeks ago and they had all this time to improve it. But at it's current state, this is essentially a "game simulator" and not an actual game. At its current price point, I can't recommend it. Maybe at $5, but not $10.

Btw, my actual game time is around 22 hrs, but my number is inflated because the time I spend playing the demo was added in. I would have 161/161 achievements instead of 160 or whatever if the game wasn't bugged.

P.S. I've put more than 131 hrs into VS (including some DLC) and haven't even come CLOSE to doing everything, so I'm not talking out my butt here.

Most positive reviews don't have more than a few hours in... don't let them fool you. The low price is worth it for the intial handful hours, but the design collapses on itself quickly to something I just can't to come back to.

The game has really great ideas. Cards need to be played in incremental mana cost order for an ever increasing multipler. Its engaging and many systems fit into this so you hit off some insane 10+ card combos (giving you 10x the effect). And this is great, because the multiplier doesn't just affect damage, but all numbers. So you can draw 10x cards, get infinte mana etc. The game is obviously tuned around this so everything comes back to that you need to get a god deck that consistently has insane card draw and mana generation capabilities to wipe everything. And here is where the prolems begn...

Leveling up is basically a bad thing. When you level up, you must pick a card Slay the Spire style (and you have limited skips). Typically, cards are bad when you draft them. Your deck consists of evolved (specific set of cards that merge into one) cards and each have 1-3 upgrades to them, levling up forces you to pick a new base card. However, the speed of levling is completely overtuned in comparison the the evolve and upgrade speed. So what happens in a run is that you must level up as quick as possible to reach your power curve, otherwise you will get destroyed (because the game is balanced around your deck is god mode). And when you reach it, you will start to blast with ease to the point where its gets boring after 1-2 floors. But the problem is that you are slowly just becoming weaker and weaker as the card bloat makes it harder to draw your good cards, and you start to run into encounters where you don't get your combos off. And not getting a combo of typically means that you run at ~5% efficiency, and you can get one shot by a regular trash mob encounter. Yes, there are skips, banishes, rerolls etc that you can upgrade to mitigate this. But why should levling up feel like a challenge I want to avoid? It just doesn't work. Getting cards should be a great experience, it isn't later in the game - and this is a card game.

Addtionally, since everything revolves around the combos, runs just takes longer, and longer to run. Quick snappier satisying runs are replaced by 6+ floors of slog where you need to repeat the same 10x combo again and again. And although there is an alluring "play all cards"  button to speed things up - cards are not played in combo order so it is a useless suicide button. And here you also have one interesting design, all bosses on higher difficulties has a "deal x damage after every 6 cards played" - every boss. So in summary, we have a game that is all about playing long chains of cards, and the act of playing cards is punished, and for winning ecounters you are rewarded by gimping the deck. I don't get it, The thought of having to start a new run has never been that unappealing - and its not because of lack of content.

And looking at the meta progression, I'm not even 0.1% in. There are so much to unlock, at the very least 100+ of succesful runs. At the point where I can't get myself to commit 45 minutes to a 45 mindless slog run and seeing how much grind there is makes it a clear no for me. Just play another card roguelike.

I guess I'll be one of the few detractors. This game is fine as a sort of passtime to just kill 30 minutes with and do a run - but ultimately I feel it's just way less fun than vampire survivors was.

I appreciate the stab at a new genre, but I also feel like the game is not a good deck builder, nor is it a good DRPG. Both of these are genres I happen to quite like so I'm ultimately left personally disappointed.

If you're like me and have been playing games like MTG since you were a kid, the deckbuilding is extremely thin. There's not a lot of card interaction, the combo system is neat but very simple, and there's very limited ways to actually tailor a deck of cards so you'll eventually be running a fat stack.

This is weird cause it makes things like leveling up actually bad - you don't actually want that much XP gain cause you'll pollute your deck with trash if you add too many cards. There is built in guard against efficient decks where if you play the same card too many times it starts to shatter and can eventually break. So it seems like all the games design is to just push you to run some fat stack of crap and just line up combos based on mana cost. There's just not a lot of thinking.

As a dungeon crawler I think it's very poor as well. The dungeons are small, the enemies are all mechanically identical - except their sprites are swapped and they have different stats. There's nothing to explore or find really, it's all on your map and each map is one screen big. The intentionally dated and pixelated graphics I don't actually think help the game at all. It also feels like all the weapons basically do the same thing.

Most DRPGs are focused on party composition, skill synergies, and front/back row positioning and none of this exists in the game at all. You basically just dump cards at some sprites and watch them die. It also sits in this grey area zone where it's easy, but you can't really just get into a "flow" state like in vampire survivors and kind of just zone out. The action is segmented and demands a constant, yet very low, level of cognitive input. To me this makes me think the game would have been better if it was just not a deck builder and instead a pure Vampire Survivors DRPG.

Ultimately I feel like the design of this game wasn't made by someone who deeply understands either genre it's taking from.

If you like it I'm not hating, but I don't think this is a game for me.

The crack cocaine of games has returned, but in an entirely different form!

Vampire Crawlers is a Roguelite deck builder, with a bit of a first person dungeon crawler twist. I mean, that's why it's called "Vampire Crawlers"! It takes some time for the game to really click, and for the brilliance to show, because at first it just seems like chaos. It's hard to figure out synergies, builds, and anything in a game where a lot of what you are theorycrafting is the cards themselves. You do have passive upgrades here, like in Vampire Survivors, and also an Arcana system that actually plays a huge role in how you craft your builds.

I mention builds because that's a major part of what I love about RPGs, Roguelites, and other such games; is theorycrafting "builds". In Vampire Crawlers, the only major flaw is your own ability to wrangle the systems and figure out how to really have fun. For instance, in due time you can unlock more slots for crawlers, who are returning characters from Vampire Survivors. These characters, these miserable piles of secrets, are your ticket to theory crafting builds ALONG with the arcana system. Pay attention to the color of cards, the mana cost of cards, and also to cards with a "w" which marks them as a wildcard which can be used to extend combos longer than they should last.

I got a hold of these systems, and the game instantly got MUCH BETTER. I can now properly theorycraft builds, and the game is amazing! I mean, it was enough that I love the music, the charming dithered 3D graphics, and silly corny voiceacting. The game was already pretty fun. But it felt like chaos at first. That's the main problem with this game, is that it is pure chaos trying to wrangle so many kinds of cards, with so many different variables INCLUDING the ability to upgrade a single card during a run and only that run. But if you figure out all these little things, the game is a delightful pixelated bit of addictive fun!

Its definitely fun. But so far, it hasn't grabbed me nearly the same way Vampire Survivors did.

Mostly because it feels a lot more grindy, in a much more repetitive way, for a lot less reward. The main loop so far is basically just: Go to the highest difficulty area you can easily survive, then repeat that over and over until you have enough gold to upgrade your stats and cards to survive the next toughest area. Then repeat.

So far, I haven't encountered that same feeling of constantly unlocking new and crazy branching paths with every single run that makes VS so addicting. Maybe that starts to happen later, and I'm just not there yet. Or maybe they're planning on expanding everything with later updates and DLC. But so far, making any progress has been just enough of a slog, that I'm starting to feel less and less motivated to keep playing.

The UI is a bit annoying, too. Its way too easy to accidentally click on the wrong card, because of how they move around constantly. navigating the town between runs is needlessly slow and awkward. And simply quitting the game is hidden away in the deep dark corner of the settings menu for some reason. None of it makes the game unplayable, but it does make it just that much more annoying than it should be.

But, I'm certainly not disappointed in the amount of fun I've had, and hopefully will still have, for the price I paid.

I'm honestly not sure why we use "addicting" as a compliment when we're talking about video games, but if there's one thing that can be told about this game is that it is addicting. Bright colors, energetic soundtrack, easy to understand card-based gameplay and pure serotonin flooding the brain every time you open a chest containing some powerful reward.

All of that is true about most trashy mobile games. The difference, I think, is that unlike those games, Vampire Crawlers doesn't try to hide it's simplicity behind a bunch of weird terminology and superfluous mechanics. The challenge is simple. Defeat hordes of enemies with seemingly exponentially growing HP. That's it. And to do that, you either have to constantly evolve your deck and play style... or find a broken card combo. It doesn't try to punish you for the latter. Honestly, it's fun either way, just like it was in Vampire Survivors.

On the surface it tries to imitate those old first-person dungeon crawlers like Wizardry and Ultima Underworld, but the dungeons themselves are only a few interconnected rooms. No mazes, no puzzles. It's more of a homage than a faithful recreation. But even this layout serves a purpose - it allows you to choose, which enemies to engage and in what sequence. Your can either plan your growth by carefully defeating each enemy on the floor before facing the Big Bad guarding the way down, or, if you're already OP as ♥♥♥♥ (or, at least, if you think you are) you can just rush through, flatten the floor boss and move on.

And speaking of growth, the deck building system is simple, but satisfying. You start with a few basic cards, but as you progress through the game, you  unlock new ones. You always start with the same deck (depending on the Crawler you chose at the beginning of the run), but you can add a new card each time you defeat a group of enemies or a floor boss. You can also evolve your cards, making them stronger and unlocking new abilities. There is a possibility to make a seemingly invincible deck that can defeat everything in one turn by drawing the same set of cards over and over again, but each repeated use of the the same card makes it "crack" until it breaks, summoning a powerful boss that will, most likely, murder you on the spot for your hubris.

(You can STILL defeat even this guy if you're tough enough. The card that broke is gone for the rest of the run though.)

So, yeah. Vampire Crawlers is a simple, colorful, energetic, and addicting game. It doesn't try to hide its trashiness behind a veil of complexity or pretentiousness. It knows what it is and embraces it wholeheartedly. Well worth your time and money.

They've only gone and done it again. Even if it doesn't blaze a trail in terms of spearheading a subgenre (Vampire Survivors has been quite the influential game, needless to say), Vampire Crawlers has proven to be a damn compelling smorgasbord of game systems.

This time around, it's a grid-based roguelite dungeon-crawler with a card-battling combat system at its core. The classic compelling loop of acquiring weapons and powerups, translated into a format more in line with Slay the Spire than an inverse bullet hell.

What's more, the mechanical aesthetics of dungeon-crawling mesh surprisingly well (yet also quite understandably) with the visual aesthetics of the Castlevania-styled world of Vampire Survivors. After all, even though Symphony of the Night is a sidescroller rather than a blobber, it and other Igavanias could arguably be considered dungeon-crawlers in their own right.

All in all, it's another great roguelite from Poncle and co. Will they give this game a fair whack of DLC like Vampire Survivors? Probably. It wouldn't surprise me if they did.

Oh wow.  I mean I love Vampire Survivors, but I didn't get too much into it because I got swept off with all the survivor clones and had fun with a lot of them.  I love card games but same thing, I play all the card games I have but never finished any of them.

I got Vampire Crawlers and it's to me, the most fun card game I've ever played.  The closest thing to this is the Neo Geo game Crossed Swords, which I didn't really enjoy back when it debuted in the arcades and on the original Neo Geo home console.

So I started this game on my PC with an Xbox controller and I love the old-style tank navigation.  turn left or right, and move forward.  Very similar to the Amiga game Dungeon Master and of course the original Tomb Raider games.  You can see a map and can select what opponents you want to fight, so it's not random encounters like you get in most RPG games, which is great!  Very turn-based and relaxing.

When you get into a fight, you can just hit a button (X on the controller) to play all your cards and you just keep mashing it if you don't know what you're doing to win the fight.  You'll learn how to conserve cards pretty early, just see if you're fighting a few opponents and you might want to select what cards to play instead of wasting all the cards to fight.  Anyway, for the treasure chests and other rewards, you can see them as you move around, and you have to move towards them to 'bump' into them to get the reward.

They brought a lot of the audio and visual effects of the original Survivors into this card game, so it gives you the same feel from the original, while it's a whole other genre.  Very, very satisfying transition from a bullet-heaven game to a card battler!

Very good follow-up to Vampire Survivors!  If you love the look and feel of Survivors, I'm pretty sure this will be another time-sucking game that you're not going to regret playing.  To be honest, I like this more than the original.  So happy for this sequel!

Crush up a whole game of Slay the Spire and mainline it, dosed with all the spicy flavours of the infamous Vampire Survivor; a game with zero vampires. 

There's none in Vampire Crawlers either, and it's because neither game sucks. If you're into deckbuilders and need something insanely addictive, the formula of the Survivors game has been shockingly well translated to card-flinging first-person dungeon-crawling, with juuuuust enough skill and thought needed to keep it from devolving into a button mashing fest. Speedy strategizing and precariously stacking combos is just as satisfying as ploughing through a crowd of enemies in Survivors, and Crawlers benefits from a few years of refinement of the studio behind these games, making it feel all the more smooth to interact with and easy to learn. As a bonus, much of your existing Vampire Survivors knowledge helps root out secrets in this game too, particularly with returning item combos.

It's a no brainer if you're into Survivor-likes. Vampire Crawlers is addictive, lightweight, and perfectly priced for what is ostensibly a spin-off genre mixing experiment. For all the flaws that could be said about it, it lives up to its heritage of being the digital equivalent of a candy bar; addictive, sugary fun... without the addition to your waistline.

Yeah it's pretty good lol.  If you liked Vampire Survivors you'll probably like Vampire Crawlers, and for the price I'd say it's extremely worth it.  There's also a free demo if you just want to test the waters and see if this game is for you.  If you enjoy the demo you will almost certainly enjoy the rest of the game.

This is a very addicting deckbuilding roguelike, think Slay the Spire if it was made on crack cocaine and also meth supplied from Heisenberg.  The game's combo system where you try and stack cards of ascending mana costs gives incredible amounts of serotonin, especially late-game when you can regularly achieve combos of over 20 cards at start dealing hundreds of thousands of damage while drawing 15 cards at a time.  Crawlers - the playable characters - all also feel distinct enough to encourage players to try out different deckbuilding goals as you explore each dungeon.  The different starting decks and passives do help make each dungeon run feel different and they're usually fun to mess with (though I have some critiques as listed later).  

The presentation was also enjoyable.  I loved seeing the 2d sprites from Vampire Survivors in a 3d environment, giving the game a sort of neo-retro feel.  The music in this game was also stylish as hell, they do the thing from modern Fire Emblem games where they have 2 versions of a music track - one quieter version during exploration where the track takes a chiller, more foreboding tone and then a battle version of the track ramps up the energy as you start chaining cards together.  They also added voice acting for the Crawlers and hearing little voice clips as you play cards helped give the characters more... uh... character.  

However, I do have some critiques.  I think the game is a little too easy to break - honestly moreso than Vampire Survivors.  Pugnalla in particular is unlocked very early on and can very easily create infinites thanks to abusing the game's combo system.  While I understand that this game isn't exactly designed to be difficult, Pugnalla feels so much stronger than every other character (besides the secret character who is unlocked after beating a Superboss) that it makes picking different characters a tough ask lol.  I managed to break the game so hard that when I was in the final level, I accidentally one-shot the final boss.  (For those curious, I used a setup with [spoiler]Gallo+Pugnalla+Poppea to max out my greed and farmed coins via overkilling bosses.  I then used the Coin Counter gem - a gem that deals damage based on how many coins you gained in a run[/spoiler], and completely obliterated the final boss).  I'm not really sure how I feel about being to do that... essentially on accident lol.  This isn't a dealbreaker by any means but this was something I had mixed feelings about so I felt like I had to mention this in my review.

I also kinda wish there was more to do after you beat the final boss lol.  I already had most achievements by the time I beat the final boss so at this point I'm just gonna have to wait for more content before picking up the game again.  That being said, for the price I paid, 26 hours of fun gameplay is more than enough and if this game gets updates like Vampire Survivors is still getting I think this "problem"/nitpick will naturally go away.

Some other nitpicks/QoL features that I hope get addressed in future updates:
*) You should be able to sort your hand by cost or something, it kinda got annoying to navigate the large hands you can naturally generate considering this game is designed to be broken.
*) You should also be able to sort your deck, for similar reasons to the above.
*) In Vampire Survivors you can see which maps you've cleared with which characters.  I think it'd be nice to get that feature in Crawlers to help improve replayability a bit.

Overall, while I do have some nitpicks, I think the game is overall very well made and would highly recommend for those looking for a chill roguelike time.

Vampire crawlers has the same combo making, horde bashing, stat increasing gameplay that Vampire survivor was known for. Any fans coming from the first game with find all the rogueLITE goodness that made the first game as addicting as it was.

THAT said, if you're joining as a card game fan, looking for a cool new card game, things might not be as great.

To put it bluntly, Vampire Crawlers gameplay is just...uninteresting. There are simply not many important decisions for the player to make. The few in-battle choices are usually very obvious and the enemies are just different pixel versions of themselves with different health/damage stats. 

While it's amusing to hit a long combo streak due to some semi-infinite builds, the cards themselves, much alike the enemies, don't really differ that much from one another.

The worst part though is the grind. At every corner you are nudged into it - healing and boss overkill gold encourage extended battles, enemies are skippable but at the cost of exp or EVEN IMPORTANT RELICS, and of course the more gold you have the more upgrades you unlock.

The lack of interesting effects on cards and enemies along with the grind makes the game very repetitive, very quickly. This might appeal to gamers who enjoy the said grind, but those looking for tactical card play might not find it here.


[h1][b]Favorite mechanics[/b][/h1]
• I liked how crawlers are special cards that both give a passive and active effect and thus unlock new combo opportunities


[h1][b]The positive[/b] [/h1] 
+ Quick to learn.

+ Decent rogueLITE mechanics


[h1][b]The neutral[/b][/h1] 
* Personally found the map navigation cumbersome as it's harder to play with just the mouse


[h1][b]The negative[/b][/h1] 
- Not much strategy involved with card play and deckbuilding

- Poor enemy and card effect variety

- Gets repetitive fast


[h1][b]Conclusion:[/b] [/h1] 
Lots of meta upgrades, but very few tactical or interesting choices to make during card play. Still somewhat fun, but gets repetitive fast. Recommended, 7/10.

I sat down with this at what I am pretty sure was early afternoon. When I looked up again it was dark outside and I had not eaten anything. I am not sure what happened in between except that at some point my deck started doing something that I can only describe as mathematically irresponsible and I simply had to keep going to see how far it would go. Vampire Crawlers takes Vampire Survivors and rebuilds it as a turn-based deckbuilder, which sounds like a combination that should not work as well as it does. You explore dungeons, pick up cards, and slowly start figuring out how they interact with each other. Then something clicks. Two cards that seemed fine separately turn out to be absolutely unhinged together, your numbers start multiplying instead of adding, and suddenly you are doing damage that belongs in a different game entirely. The run that I thought would take twenty minutes took three hours. I have no regrets. The roguelike structure keeps things moving and the way each run can go in a completely different direction depending on what cards show up means you are never really doing the same thing twice. It does reuse some assets from Vampire Survivors, which if you have a thousand hours in that game might sting a little. And card sorting is not in yet which will bother you by hour two. But at ten euros it is one of the easiest purchases I have made this year. The depth is real, the loop is satisfying, and I genuinely did not notice the sun going down.

Lots of fun so far! Many elements carried over from Vampire Survivors, so people familiar with that game should have an easy time understanding the mechanics in this game. The deckbuilding, customization, and fights feel straightforward but have enough nuance to make you think about the decisions you're making. Paying attention to combos and card/crawler interactions is immediately rewarding so your progression isn't so painfully tied to gold and progression from the hub. The fights are quick enough that you bounce from one to the next, but each individual stage still takes a decent amount of time to clear through if you aren't just gunning it for the exit each time. I like the dungeon crawling mechanics. The map is straightforward and easy to interpret, and you're not obligated to turn the camera if you're a speed demon. 
The only thing that feels weird, especially bouncing between STS2 and this, is that you only need to click on your cards to use them instead of moving them forward from your hand. I think it's really conducive to the way this game is intended to feel, though, so not really a negative for this game in my opinion.
It's clear that the developers put a lot of love into this game. Highly recommend picking this up and trying it out for at least a few hours, if not seeing it through to the end.

As someone who loves both deck builders and rouge-likes, this scratched an itch for the time I spent with it. However I do have some complaints with the game that made runs feel tedious.

I think what made Vampire Survivors fun was how fairly hands off it was for how simple and rewarding it felt. For me personally though, it was not engaging enough to justify grinding gold for permanent upgrades. I think auto attacking games like Vampire Survivors and Brotato are executed well, just not something I hold highly and would experience wise would equate to more engaging Cookie Clicker.

Part of why I was excited for this game was hoping it'd be a tad more engaging and strategical with building a deck for runs and I think for the most part the early game well in a way that stays true to Vampire Survivors. I was not expecting the level of gameplay here to be as strategical as Balatro or Slay the Spire and I think that is perfectly okay! However, there is a quick threshold I think the gameplay loop becomes so extremely easy and broken that trivializes the game in a way that I found it to be more inconvenient to play than rewarding.

Each Dungeon has a set of floors with a set amount of combat encounters, some optional and some are required to get to the Boss to finish the floor. Some cards you can acquire boost your stats like Hand Size, Projectile Amount, Strength, and Splash Damage for that single combat then resets when its over. There are also stats that can be upgraded that stay through the whole dungeon, such as Luck or XP Gain but for now I will just focus on the per combat stats. These stats can be permanently upgraded through gold in the hub menu before your runs.

The combat loop involves setting up your character stat wise to clear the encounter, the game also rewards you for creating combos cards. A strength card might give 10% more strength and cost 1 mana but if you chain it after a 0 cost card it will give you more strength. This is actually a fairly fun and engaging system however it becomes extremely tedious to do this in later encounters as fights scale later in the game especially when you have a broken build and the auto-play button does not always do the best job at optimally playing. Fights take ages to set up and with no risk it becomes very tedious, very quickly. I truly think this could be fixed if temp stats reset after every floor instead of each combat and have the fights scale higher to accommodate for this so its still somewhat engaging. Enemies will hit like a truck unless you take the time to set up a large combo to deal as much damage as possible in a single turn, especially during some bosses that counter every card draw.

While I understand the main appeal to Vampire Survivors is the simplicity in the "auto" aspect of that game I think it works fell for how hands off that game. I think that gameplay philosophy creates inherent tension in a game that demands this level of engagement. Once you snowball it becomes so easy. I am hopeful for the game's future and would likely revisit it if there is any DLC.

Some other general thoughts:
-Wild cards are beyond busted and trivialize the game
-The hub menu needs a UI fix to easily select any of the buildings without linearly scrolling through them
-The game needs a better way to navigate/sort larger hand sizes
-I would love a reset run button

I've sunk embarrassing hours into Slay the Spire, Balatro, Monster Train... you know the type. Vampire Crawlers just joined that list and it got there fast. And maybe... to the top of it.

The run length is basically perfect. Long enough to feel satisfying, short enough that you immediately want to go again. The in-run progression has that beautiful snowball curve where things start clicking and suddenly you're doing way more than you should be, and the meta-progression keeps the whole thing moving forward between sessions. Both loops feel intentional, not padded.

Most importantly: the gameplay is just spot on right. Turn-based, but never slow. The Turboturn mechanic keeps things punchy, and building your deck around a busted combo and then watching it actually pop off is that specific flavor of dopamine that this genre lives and dies by. Vampire Crawlers delivers it in spades.

At $9.99 this is an absolute no-brainer. The Poncle team has done it again. This could easily be a $29.99 game and I'd still recommend it. Sleeper GOTY candidate, no question.

Highly, highly recommended.

It's okay. The videogame equivalent of cake - tastes good, but is bad nutritionally. Depends what you expect from something that deems itself a card game.

Scratches that part of your brain that likes to see numbers go up, but there's not much of substance behind it. Your performance is dictated purely by the first room. If you make it past a few encounters in the start of a run and get a good rune or +hand size buff, you'll be quickly on your way to some build that devolve to slamming down your entire deck and watching everything explode. Resource management and build planning, what's that? Builds that let you cycle through your entire deck with constant drawing are not only easily accomplished, but the optimal strategy considering the endless mana you'll be swimming in.

The game is also build around grinding, both from the number of encounters and how mechanics are built. A good example is the arcana (relic?) that grants you 1 permanent mana every 100 cards you play, or runes that grant you luck. You're basically encouraged to drag out fights in the start while everything is doing manageable damage, to beef yourself up for later.

Vampire Survivors, but it's a card roguelite instead of a reverse bullet hell.

Just absolutely spectacular in every way. Each run feels like you're progressing meaningfully in the big picture, even if that particular run didn't go as planned. It's just as addicting as Vampire Survivors, and you can find yourself easily losing hours in that familiar "just one more run" sort of way.

Edit: After playing for ~50 hours and unlocked all the achievements, I can still safely say that this game is very nearly perfect if you're interested in rogue-likes, deck-builders, or dungeon crawlers. The runs can vary deeply depending on the crawler(s) you've chosen and the cards you prioritize. It retains many of the systems that Vampire Survivors implemented, such as the ability to limit your power-up pool to increase the likelihood of creating your ideal build and the ability to COMPLETELY break the game once you've cleared a few late-game hurdles. Without entering spoiler territory, once you've unlocked a couple of arcana and special gemstones, you can essentially create endless combos, stacking your hp, armor, gold, and crawler duration to the moon. The only limit, in theory, is how long you're interested in looping each hand to farm. 

I, unreasonably, wish that there was more to uncover, collect, and exploit after the final achievement has unlocked, but, realistically, the game cost $10 and gave me 50 awesome hours. If Vampire Crawlers receives even half of the post-launch content that Survivors boasted, we'll still be eating real, real good for a long time. Personally, I'm hoping for collabs with Slay the Spire 2 and Balatro, but, no matter what it is, I'm sure Poncle has got something exciting in the works.

TLDR: Buy this game, play it, break it, and share it.

Writing this review after beating the seemingly last boss of the last area (as of Release) --- this game is literally digital crack 2.0~ Don't buy it if you're not into the idea of losing a good couple of hours of your precious time without realizing that the week is already over and you haven't gotten any sh*t done. To be fair though: it's not as long of an experience as other titles that try to keep you hooked for 100s of hours. It's a concise romp of sheer joy with some light frustration mixed in to keep it interesting.

It's easy to point towards a game like this and state "it's just numbers go up dopamine slop" - but that's a rather uninformed statement. Making an addicting and fun gameplay loop with a multitude of interacting systems and make it make sense for the long haul without disregarding the experience from a new players perspective is an incredibly difficult task and anyone that ever tried game dev for a while will agree with me on that. The start is a bit slow but you can already guess at what's to come and the game delivers on that 10-fold. The rewards all seem fair and the pace of unlocking new stuff is pretty fast. The moment you really get a good build going is a pure gold rush of a good time and the game just keeps giving you more options to do so.

While this probably won't spawn a new genre like Survivors did, it is an amazingly put together mix of a traditional dungeon crawler (which have mostly come out of fashion these days) and a roguelike deckbuilder that isn't just another StS-clone while offering enough fresh ideas to elevate both sides to a new level. Highly recommended~

Side note: The only bugs I encountered were minor glitches with menues and that the game can be a bit wonky with detecting your main screen (& res) on a multi-monitor-setup. Nothing major or game breaking. Oh, and please let me end the game from the tavern menu... 😅

Short version: Bump into monster, play cards, monsters explode into gems, get more cards, repeat.

Longer version: Vampire Crawlers is indeed very much Vampire Survivors meets turn based card game. In the same way that Survivors was a wonderfully inverted version of a bullet hell formula, Crawlers plays fast and loose with card battle systems. Yes, there are mana and health pools. Yes cards have a cost to play. But within minutes of playing the game you'll see how combos just flow and limited resources are sort of a suggestion more than a rule. I wouldn't say the game is particularly challenging by any means, and getting a "great" run isn't really mandatory. Instead, most cards can largely be built into a winning formula with just the slightest consideration when building out your deck. 

It probably comes down to this, do you want to play a Vampire Survivors card battler? If so, you'll enjoy this.

Definitely just as captivating as the original, but in card game form! 
Lots of fun combos for cards, as well as the combo mechanic itself, make deckbuilding interesting and adaptive. The gem system is also a fun way to make things even more varied (think micro-buffs/changes to one instance of a card), and are a good way to teach the importance of deck-thinning while also having a system in place for combos that get [i]too[/i] infinite. The music is great and the VA work is charming. Definitely worth the money, and I'm excited to see if more stuff comes out.

P.S. You can still kill the red death at the end of a run!

Kinda sad they went the card battler route. That said this game is awesome. For 10 dollars I beat the game in 30 hours. I didn't rush but I wasn't going slow ether. It's everything you'd expect from the original game just with some twists. DLC will be insta buy.

Lightning can strike twice. Vampire Crawlers not only recaptures the magic of Vampire Survivors, but the game also completely improves upon its foundation in nearly every aspect. Utilizing and implementing [b]familiar concepts[/b] with individuality allowed them to appeal to veterans of the series while still emphasizing fresh iterations for newcomers. The card mechanics are abundant, menu interactions are fluid, and pixel animations remain smooth without lagging. As for improvements over the former, your [b]decisions matter[/b] more often, the difficulty remains balanced throughout, visual clarity is preserved despite insane combinations, and its [b]medium-length[/b] does not result in an overabundance of content. [h2]Crack: The video game. 9/10[/h3]

[h3]PROS:[/h3]

- Vampire Survivors in Dungeon Crawler Mode. I didnt think it would work... it works VERY VERY well!
- Graphics are charming and well done.
- Works perfectly on Linux. Quite a bit better than windows from my testing.
- Music is a banger. I usually shut the music off on most of these games but this is turned UP.
- Fast games. Usually 10-20 mins per run. Awesome!
- Tons of unique synergies
- Awesome evolutions that use the previous games setups. For instance  Garlic + Heart. Works in both versions. Sorry if you find that to be a spoiler.
- Jim Sterling is gone!!! Im an part of the moniker. He is a notorious grifter and horrid person all around. Been at the center of a lot of drama over the years and has all but been ousted from the community as a whole. Just a horrid person in general. As soon as i saw he was no longer involved I bough the game.

[h3]CONS:[/h3]

- Game is VERY easy. I have yet to lose a single run and im getting builds such as +600% damage, +700% duration, +650% XP. Like just WILD builds. I had a build that every turn i was getting 20-30 mana and I JUST started. (Get armor and GGEZ Clap. Congrats on your W.)
- Jimothy Sterlingus' skidmarks are still in the game. I did laugh VERY hard when i heard Geovannas voice though. If that wasnt done on purpose as a jab that is even more hilarious. Thank you for that. 
- The crawling is kinda annoying. Specifically the having to turn like tank controls when moving. I have found it easier to just stare at the minimap and use it to navigate around since there doesnt seem to be negative consequences for doing so that I have found.
- Achievements are way too easy to get. I did 1 run and got 17 achievements... lol. Im sure that will change as i get deeper into the game but yeah... If i kept playing im sure I would have beaten the game in one sitting which is why I decided to take a break for a day or two.

[h3]CONCLUSION:[/h3]

Fantastic game. I was one of the first people to post a red death kill in the original game. I had a 48 hour long run time I posted to reddit but it has since been removed due to reddits insanity. (Reddit is a horrid place filled with very... "simple"... people caught in an echo-chamber. I will see if i can find my original play-through on my old YT account and can upload it somewhere in the community area.) Enough about that hellscape. 

All that to say, I loved that game until Jim "Worlds #1 Skidmark" Sterling got involved in the game. I dropped it then and there and refused to play anymore of Poncles games until he was gone. Yes his stain is in VCrawlers but removing those characters would cause more issues than doing what they did, which was making the characters he touched be basically useless in comparison to everyone else and the voice. That voice cracked my best friend and I up when we heard it. Nicely done. Dunno if that was on purpose but if it wasnt as stated prior, thats even funnier. 

This reinvigorated my love of roguelikes and dungeon crawlers and sent me down a path of finding a ton of indie crawlers and buying those to play after I beat this. 

I am extremely excited about what is to come next from Poncle. They are a master at these "Braindead Clickers". I mean no ill will with that statement. I have heard this said by lots of people and they mean it doesnt require you to get a full guide out to play as something like Persona 5 Royal would. Its just an easy pop-in pop-out kinda game and that has its place in the ecosystem as well. 

Amazing game and cannot wait to play another run!

Honestly, I am enjoying the game. However, I haven’t found it to be as difficult or as long as I would have liked. I’m already around four-fifths of the way through the achievements, and while the gameplay loop is still engaging, it’s starting to drag on… and on. It’s nice to see all the items and heroes from Vampire Survivors, though.

That said, I do wish there was more in the base game than what’s currently available in version 1.0. I’m sure more content will come down the line with DLC and updates, but right now it does feel a bit lacking given how hyped the game was. To be fair, though, considering the amount of content, secrets, and DLC in Vampire Survivors, it would have been difficult to meet those expectations right out of the gate.

The dungeons feel very small and empty. It doesn’t really feel like you’re exploring a new area—each area and each level within it feels too similar. There’s no real sense of “OMG, where am I? What is this? What’s happening?” like there is in Vampire Survivors, which is disappointing. Even finding things like coffins and relics feels very underwhelming, and that’s honestly the biggest letdown of the game for me.

On the technical side, the game runs perfectly on my Steam Deck OLED, which is always a plus.

Honest take (as a fan of Vampire Survivors): the game is enjoyable, and if you like the developer’s other titles, you should pick it up. But if you’re new to the series, I’d recommend waiting a while for more content to be added before paying full price. It’s fun, but in my opinion, it will always live in the shadow of Vampire Survivors… that game was peak.

There's a surprising amount of meat on this games bones. I'm not sure if it's going to have the same support cycle as Survivors, but what's there is fun, the progression feels great, and there's a lot of ways to go about breaking the game.
Going infinite is assumed by the game. It's exceptionally easy to go infinite, and if the game was just about achieving infinity over and over again, it wouldn't be very fun. While in Slay the Spire, it's cool to have a run that turns into a game of solitaire, if it were to consistently happen every run, that wouldn't be terribly fun. If you've ever played a tabletop game with someone that goes infinite, you know how quickly the power of god can invalidate the entire game.
Therefore, Crawlers has a couple of mechanics that limit infinity to a more tolerable number, around five hundred or so. If you repeatedly play a card, it starts to shatter. Play a shattered card too many times, and it breaks, removing it from your deck and summoning a reaper enemy. As long as you end your turn, your cards repair yourself and can be combo'd again. If you're going infinite, which again, the game is assuming and built around, it wants you to go BIG. you aren't going to kill enemies one damage at a time like you're a blood artist deck in MTG, you need to be making escalating-ly large attacks, because if you don't, every action you take will add an eye to a boss' action gauge, and once it fills, it will take its action and load another one, so you aren't just playing a game of solitaire. While hoards of enemies are easily swept away, bosses are always able to at least touch you, which makes going infinite a far more interesting and interactive prospect, because you need to be efficiently infinite to survive some of the later bosses that can just dump damage every couple of plays.
I've seen some people complain about these mechanics, but to me, they're what make the game interesting to begin with. without them, this game would be woefully boring and easy. I also prefer going BIG over going infinite.
The only complaint I have from this game, aside from disliking the town because everything feels too many clicks away, is the evolution system. It's practically the same as Survivors, find a weapon(s) and its accessory, open a chest by killing a boss, and make the fused weapon. In Survivors, what limited weapon fusion was that you had to max out the level of each weapon, and there was a chance you wasted an inventory slot on a weapon or accessory that you don't have the space to fully evolve. In Crawlers, you don't need to upgrade weapons, nor do you need to worry about inventory space. What you do need to worry about is the rarity of the evolution gem that enables fusion. I have had runs where I only got a single evolution gem, and I have beaten the final level, dealing 2.5 million damage to the final boss in a single hit (the damage equal to gold gem is insane), and I did not have A SINGLE evolved weapon for that run. I've reduced the rarity of the evolution gem to common, meaning that theoretically, it should show up more often than not, but I've had no such luck, in fact, it's become a lot rarer and I have no idea why.
Another issue I have is that once unlocked and used, the system where you increase or decrease the rarity of gems (or outright seal them) is ludicrously expensive, even if I make 2.5 million a run. There's also no way (to my knowledge) to reset the rarity rates, meaning that going forward, every decision I make regarding adjustments to the gems rarity needs to be carefully weighed, because it's going to be a massive grind if I want to shake things up or try different builds. Poncle, please add a refund option for the gem rarities like you do with the upgrades.
Overall, Vampire Crawlers is great. It's a good deal pricier than Survivors, but you'll find a more meaty, meaningful experience here, but it doesn't have the sheer breadth of content that Survivors has grown to have over the past couple of years. But, if Survivors is any metric to go by, investing in Crawlers can prove to be a worthwhile endeavor as more meaningful content is added. Fingers crossed we get another Castlevania DLC.

Love the game, but it crashed my PC (pretty sure it overheated my GPU because there is no frame / refresh rate limit) and I had a corrupted save upon loading it again, losing 8 hours of progress.  I had no other option but to start again at the tutorial.  That's just not acceptable in any way, devs... especially seeing as how there are reports of this dating back to months before the game was released.

Hopefully, the save corruption issue gets addressed so it doesn't keep happening to other people.  If you do happen to crash while playing, I'd highly recommend that the first thing you do (before trying to launch it again, which will sync your potentially corrupted save to the Steam cloud) is go to the Steam Cloud remote storage page and download your most recently synced files as a backup.  You'll probably still lose some progress, but not all of it.

I think this game is rated high because of the price. As a card game it seems cool at first but it's almost always obvious which cards you should play in which order most of the time. The randomness of the cards your given makes it feel too much like winning or losing isn't much in your control. There's no difference between the enemies other than what they look like and their hp. It's another one of those games where you cannot win without losing over and over again to gain enough permanent upgrades to finally win the game. Why not start the player off with the potential to win the entire game from the start? Because these type of games are good at giving the illusion that you stand a chance so you waste your time losing  over and over again. The game just trickles in little upgrades and bonuses to help you over time so that eventually you can win. You do a run and get enough money that the next run you can have like 1 starting armor...1...Then there's the bridge which makes it even more obvious. If you don't have enough upgrades once you get to the bridge you're forced to replay older stages over and over until you are able to pass it. I have no idea how soo many people gobble this game up. I think becuase it's cheap a lot of the reviews are from children...

Finished everything that one can finish after about 24 hours of playing. Overall: Fun experience, very reminiscient of classics in the genre like Slay the Spire, although vastly easier. Early on, you will die ever so often, which is to be expected, but once you got everythign unlocked in the hub, it's really getting funky. Love the mechanic of 'breaking cards' if you use them too often in a turn, since that 
1. gives oyu a chance to reduce your decks size if needed
