Poncle does it again.  This is a fantastic deckbuilder with the kind of grindy meta-progression that makes Vampire Survivors so addicting.  Vampire Crawlers is immediately super addicting and has that terrible "just 1 more run" psychological effect that the best deckbuilders and roguelikes have.  On one hand this current launch version of the game can be 100%'d in around 20 hours if you are very experienced with Vampire Survivors and also deckbuilding concepts, but on the other hand this game is absolutely going to be the baseline upon which multiple free updates and paid DLCs are added: just like Vampire Survivors before it.

The graphics are wonderfully gritty with great spell effects and general crunchiness.

The UI is clean and everything controls well with good options for controller, keyboard, or my preferred style of just using the mouse and clicking your cards with keyboard movement in the dungeons.

The music is absolutely incredible.  We now live in a world where I can be nostalgic for Vampire Survivors music and happy to hear remixes of it.  Shoutout to Yoko Shimomura who was brought in as a guest composer for the Vampire Crawlers main theme: she killed it as usual.

Story: What are you, high?

Gameplay: I'd rather not spoil anything, you should play it for yourself.

Verdict: Vampire Crawlers is an easy recommend especially for the price.  It isn't trying to compete in playtime and depth with games like Slay The Spire II or HADES II, but it is every bit their equal in one important area: how much fun you have while playing it.

TLDR: If you like Meta Progression buffs.... this is a solid game at launch. If you dislike meta progression just go back to playing Binding of Isaac or even Brotato if you prefer the survivor like genre. 

I think this game does what it sets out to do well, but I can not recommend it to players due to my own preferences. If you like Meta progression in your games where you get more powerful between runs by spending earned currency to make your accounts base strength higher than this will probably scratch an itch for you. I find the meta progression sort of defeats that feeling of accomplishment as you end up with abilities that improve your damage, add health regen, add armor (damage mitigation) making runs easier and easier and ultimately "unlocking" the ability to go to harder maps/regions. There is a bridge map that is sort of a meta progress check where you can't really win unless you have achieved certain meta progression thresholds. If you understand the game systems you may be able to beat the bridge with less meta progression as a smooth brain like myself but the point still stands that meta progression gates your access to the game. I prefer roguelikes (I know this is a "survivorlike") with no meta progression outside of potentially unlocking ways to play. Binding of Isaac being a classic example.

Game starts out slow enough. You get a lot of simple card synergies early on that you can trial and error before the game gives you gem slotting. This mechanic is fun to mix and match however as you scale up you will need to start locking in and finding the most advantageous combos. This is where it clicked for me. 
The game has many small systems in play at all times. Cards are colored and certain colors get benefits depending on the character you're playing as. If you play the cards in ascending mana cost you get a combo bonus that multiplies your dmg/stat boost. You can combine cards with a specific gem that makes new, stronger cards. If you played Vampire Survivors then the combos will already be familiar to you. 
Traversing the map is simple. I found my xbox controller was the most intuitive way to navigate the simple mazes. You interact with objects and monsters by running into them. 
All in all, I'm having a blast.

I think the best descriptor of this game is "Slay the Spire on crack".

If you've played Vampire Survivors and/or Slay the Spire, you likely know what to expect from this game: a mana system, synergies between cards, meta-progression, flashy effects, and the eventual breakdown of the game's entire balance. It plays like a pretty standard deckbuilder at first, just with a neat combo mechanic attached, but it will quickly devolve into the madness of letting a combo go on for so long that the enemies rarely get the chance to take their turn. It's a very fun time, though one that may alienate those who want to use more nuanced strategies like in StS. If you're looking for a challenge, you will not find it here after a certain point.

Regardless, if you want a more casual take on this sort of game or are a fan of Vampire Survivors, I'd highly recommend this game! Just be aware it doesn't have nearly as much content as VS, though free content updates have been promised.

41h to complete it and it is like £8. That's good value as heck. Has a pretty easy to understand premise and is quite addictive. It's quite a dopamine ride for sure...

HOWEVER, as good as this game is, and it is very good and deserves it's high rating... there are some things I would change about it, which would make the experience even better. Slight spoilers for the game below (but it's not a story heavy game).
[spoiler]
[b]Overall Length![/b]
This is a great little game for the price of it, no doubt. But the levels are based off the levels from the Vampire Survivors game. However, quite a few later levels are missing from the game. As great as the game is for the price, I would have loved to have paid a bit more for the rest.

[b]Dungeon Length![/b]
So in each level/floor you have to clear out the enemeis and boss, and then move to the next floor. Eventually when you beat the final level, you win that area. The levels start with four floors, and rise up to six on the top levels. The problem for me, is from five floors up, the actual time to complete the whole dungeon is somewhere between 1h to 2.5h. I would have liked there to be shorter 2/3 floor dungeons at the start, and the later levels for each area rising up.

[b]Difficulty![/b]
After the levels in the first two areas, there is a bridge. This bridge is TOUGH! But go away, finish some other areas, and it will soon become a lot easier with some of the unlocks. The problem is, no area after that was really hard for me. While the dungeon bosses and mini-boss battles were always tough, the regular enemy groups became basically formalities, easy to beat, but time consuming. I would have preferred the Base enemies to scale up from floor 1 to the end a lot more, making actually getting to the end an actual challenge to overcome too.

[b]Leaderboards![/b]
A leaderboard system would really encourage competetive play between people, with a global and freinds-only mode, I could see people finding a ton of replayability in this game.
[/spoiler]

These things being said, it's still an absolute banger for the price, so I cannot really complain too much!

Sadly fails at being a good deck builder or a good 'number go up' game. 
There is 0 skill expression and everything feels meaningless as it's almost impossible to lose. Within a few hours you're able to just spam pressing "Play All" and win every encounter. Every single enemy and encounter feels the exact same and nothing that you get in your run feels unique or interesting. With proper balancing and more thought out design there would probably be something here, but as it stands it feels interesting for the first few hours until you "see through it" and realize that there's nothing more waiting for you on the other side. 
Part of the appeal of games like this is that you should be able to play them for dozens of hours and still find new fun combinations and mechanics which make you want to keep playing to try out, even if at the end of the day it's "pointless". 
In contrast, there is a massive lack of depth and variety in this game which makes it get stale very fast. 
At the end of the day the game is a:
Deck builder, which doesn't require you to think about your deck. 
Dungeon crawler, with no tension or strategy.
If this was made by another developer it would be largely ignored, in my opinion.

Game is very fun overall, has some novel mechanics for a deckbuilder and has a nice flow at the beginning. Its impressive how well they managed to catch the "Vampire"-vibe in a rogue-lite deckbuilder.

Depending on how many hours you play its a recommendation. However, soon you will have figured out the mechanics and then unfortunately every run looks the same. There are many "Crawlers" you can play as, and they have interesting different mechanics. 

However, once you figured out THE strategy, every run will look almost the same. There is almost no real decision making during a run, the game is too easy - or rather its too easy to "break the game". 

They included a mechanic that prevents you from breaking the game - mainly to prevent you from "going infinite". But: The mechanic doesn't really stop you, at best it makes you end the turn and continue on the next. At best it makes the fight last longer, but it doesn't make it more interesting. And even if the mechanic would actually work like intended and prevent you from winning easily, it would (in my personal opinion) be kind of a "lazy" solution to the problem.

I understand that balancing is very difficult, but as the game stand right now, after playing for a few hours, every run will look exactly the same. Its very casual, which is fine, but I was hoping that more thought is put into how the game can remain interesting after a few hours. 

Maybe comparing doesn't make sense, but for example Slay the Spire 2 has launched in an early access phase, and the devs are very eager to re-balance the game to provide a game that will remain interesting even after thousands of runs. 
I hope the devs of Vampire Crawlers have a similar mindset for this game as well, even though it didn't launch in early access. I think this game can become something great if more time is put into balancing and mechanics.

Very creative ideas that I've not seen before in deckbuilding, while staying very true to its Vampire Survivor roots. It has a low skill floor but I'm still trying to find where the ceiling is. Does a good job of preventing infinite from being the end-all-be-all as cards played too much in a single turn risk shattering and bosses partway through the fight can transition to a turn-counter that lets them move every couple of card plays. At the same time, it doesn't feel like an arbitrary punishment for trying to go infinite, it just balances things out. 

The combo system incentivizing a mixture of mana value cards is also incredibly nice in adding complexity. Reduce mana is often one of the least appealing choices to put on a card when I'm building a deck, and increasing the mana cost purely for the sake of comboing sometimes feels necessary and rewarding.

I'm very pleased with how creative this developer is.

I recommend this game but those purchasing it should know what they're getting into.

This is a deck building dungeon crawler similar to Slay the Spire, and some of the mechanics will be familiar. Where STS approaches (and defined, really) the genre with mechanical depth, VC takes a different tact.

There are not a ton of different card effects. There are a decent selection of cards but not so many that you're unlikely to see a particular one on a given run. The different runs can begin to feel kinda samey.

Something that this game does differently and that I found very interesting was the combo system. If you play cards in order of mana cost, you'll start building a multiplier that, ahem, multiplies the effect of subsequent cards. This is the core of the entire game, if you do not abuse the combo multiplier you will die on later runs. While the system is interesting and at times satisfying, once you figure out how to game it it kinda falls flat. 

This is due to the Wildcard. There are a number of cards you can get, some temporary deck additions and some permanent, that allow you to extend your multiplier. I found that on nearly every run, the best deck I could build was one that had many wildcards, a fair amount of draw power (which you can get either through cards or through your individual character trait), and energy (Mana, in this game) generation. With the way the wildcards work, if you can build up some combo multiplier, you can then use your draw cards to essentially cycle your whole deck.

A card that typically allows you to draw 2, for example, very quickly becomes draw 12 with enough combo. The same can be said for mana. There are three cards in particular (all books) that provide mana. The most expensive of this is a 3 cost for 4 mana return. Except with combo, you can easily push that 4 into the 20 range. Now you have high combo, high mana, and if you run low on cards, you just play another draw card to cycle your entire deck. I could build a deck that allows this on basically every run once I figured the formula out.

There is meta progression and different characters to unlock. If you are the type of person who enjoys intentionally playing sub-optimally (think like naked level 1 runs in Elden RIng) to challenge yourself, I think there's a fair amount of replayability here. But the game provides no extrinsic motivation to do so. The unlocks can all be accomplished without setting specific limitations for yourself. 

That is not to say I did not enjoy the game. It WAS fun and I enjoyed the time I spent with it (beat the last level on my first try, using above strat, in about 17 hours). The formula is satisfying. I just don't see myself coming back to it without a balance pass on deck cycling via combo abuse or some additional achievements that require playing in a certain way. Overall definitely worth the 10 bucks I spent on it, just don't expect to get STS or Monster Train levels of replayability out of it.

Just fantastic. They took V.Survivors and transformed it: keeping strengths, but developing it in a powerful way and now in the mantle of a new genre. 
I have not seen all that much of the game yet, but I already love the visual concepts and design philosophy. So while the game on the surface looks like 'just pixel graphic', my impression is that it is a quite smart and elegant 3d engine tricking you into seeing it as 2d pixel style, in a good way of course.

Gameplay: just fantastic, fun, addicting.. many elements of V.Survivors, but adding new concepts.

Vampire Crawlers: The Turbo Wildcard from Vampire Survivors, developed by poncle in collaboration with Nosebleed Interactive and published by poncle, takes the familiar identity of Vampire Survivors and reshapes it into something far more methodical and strategic. Rather than building on the fast-paced, real-time chaos of the original, this spin-off pivots toward a turn-based, deckbuilding dungeon crawler structure. It’s a bold shift that could easily have felt disconnected, but instead it manages to preserve the core appeal of stacking power, breaking systems, and watching builds spiral into absurdity—only now through careful planning rather than reflexes.

The game unfolds from a first-person dungeon-crawling perspective, where players navigate multi-layered environments filled with enemies, events, and branching paths. Combat is entirely card-driven, with each turn defined by the cards drawn and the order in which they are played. At the center of this system is the “Turbo Wildcard” mechanic, which rewards chaining cards in ascending cost to amplify their effects. This creates a surprisingly engaging rhythm: rather than simply playing the strongest card available, players are encouraged to think ahead, sequence their actions, and build momentum within a single turn. When it works well, it gives combat a sense of flow that feels both strategic and dynamic.

What carries over most effectively from Vampire Survivors is the sense of escalation. Just as the original game allowed players to evolve weapons into devastating tools of destruction, Vampire Crawlers translates that philosophy into its deckbuilding. Cards can interact in powerful ways, creating synergies that quickly transform a modest setup into something overwhelming. Runs often begin with limited options and cautious play, but as upgrades accumulate, they can spiral into explosive combinations that trivialize entire encounters. This progression—from fragile beginnings to near invincibility—is where the game feels most aligned with its roots, capturing that same addictive loop of growth and experimentation.

The roguelite structure reinforces this loop by ensuring that every run contributes to long-term progress. New characters, cards, and modifiers are gradually unlocked, expanding the range of possible strategies and encouraging repeated attempts. Even unsuccessful runs feel productive, as they open the door to new combinations or refine the player’s understanding of the system. This steady drip of rewards keeps the experience engaging, particularly for players who enjoy optimizing builds and discovering unexpected interactions.

Visually and stylistically, the game remains closely tied to its predecessor. The pixel art is vibrant and expressive, filled with familiar enemies and exaggerated effects that maintain the playful gothic tone of the series. Despite the shift in perspective and gameplay, it never loses its identity, which helps anchor the experience for returning players. The presentation is clean and readable, ensuring that even during complex turns, the player can easily track what’s happening.

That said, the game is not without its flaws. The early hours can feel somewhat slow, with limited card options and fewer opportunities for meaningful synergy. It takes time before the systems fully open up, and until they do, the experience can feel restrained compared to what it eventually becomes. Once more mechanics and cards are unlocked, the game improves significantly, but the initial pacing may test the patience of some players.

Repetition is another factor that becomes more noticeable over extended play. While the variety of cards and builds offers room for experimentation, the overall structure of dungeon runs does not change dramatically. Encounters, progression paths, and objectives follow a consistent pattern, which can make longer sessions feel predictable. Additionally, balance can be uneven—some builds become overwhelmingly powerful, reducing the need for careful planning, while others struggle to reach the same level of effectiveness. This inconsistency is part of the game’s identity, but it can occasionally undermine the sense of challenge.

Despite these issues, Vampire Crawlers succeeds in carving out a distinct place for itself. It doesn’t try to replicate the immediacy or simplicity of Vampire Survivors, instead offering a more thoughtful, slower-paced alternative that still captures the essence of building toward overwhelming power. It rewards players who enjoy experimenting with systems, planning their moves, and gradually mastering complex interactions.

In the end, Vampire Crawlers: The Turbo Wildcard from Vampire Survivors is a creative and engaging spin-off that proves the flexibility of its source material. It may not have the instant accessibility or universal appeal of the original, but it offers a deeper, more strategic experience for those willing to invest the time. By trading speed for deliberation, it creates a different kind of satisfaction—one rooted in planning, synergy, and the thrill of watching a carefully constructed build come to life.

Vamapire Crawlers manages to marry the disparate parts of several other genres into one of the most satisfying deck builders I've ever played. 

It takes the bullet heaven power fantasy of its parent game, the deck building strategy & rogue-lite elements of Slay the Spire, the classic dungeon-crawling aesthetic of Eye of the Beholder, and merges them into something that ends up greater than the sum of its parts. I bought the game at launch and it has easily become my favourite "just one more run" game, surpassing even Slay the Spire 2 and Halls of Torment (both awesome games, go check them out if you haven't).

When the creators of the phenomenal Vampire Survivors take on the turn-based card roguelike genre, you expect nothing less than gripping madness. And the new game delivers exactly what it promises: concentrated fun, familiar pixelated faces and total chaos onscreen.

From the very first minute the game grabs you and doesnt let go. Blasting through enemy-infested dungeons feels incredibly invigorating for a turn-based game. You assemble your unpredictable deck search for synergies and prepare for the main event - the TURBO DRIVE mechanic. This isnt just a marketing ploy. It allows you to wipe out entire hordes of those alltoofamiliar enemies in mere moments. The thrill of unleashing a powerful combo and watching the damage cascade is simply indescribable. The game delivers a great dopamine rush and is genuinely fun.

However behind the flashy façade lie two significant problems that could ruin the experience for seasoned fans of the genre. First, theres the criminally short campaign. The game ends just when ur just getting into the swing of things. It frankly lacks the depth and length to become a time-stealer for hundreds of hours like its predecessor.

The second and most significant Achilles' heel of the project is the overabundance of meta decks. The game is literally overflowing with broken mechanics. If you have even a passing understanding of deck building, you'll quickly figure out the right combinations. When certain parameters are met, some decks become completely unbeatable. Enemies simply dont have time to do anything, and the game becomes a chore, where you monotonously play the same unbalanced cards. The challenge is completely lost.

The end result is an excellent, vibrant, and fun attraction for a couple of evenings. If you want to relax, throw cards at hordes of monsters and feel like an all-powerful dungeon-crawlee the game will give you a lot of fun. But if you're looking for a well-balanced game and a challenging experience that will last for months, be prepared for the invincible decks here to quickly kill any interest in repeat runs.

Game is really really awesome. You can make builds and combos for any playstyle you like. Even if you don't like deck-building or strategy or RNG or RPG's... Dungeon Crawling is the only thing you can't avoid with a build, though I wouldn't put past this game at this point. xD You can just build a brute-forcer. Or go straight for the chests. You can break the game (in a good way that makes you a god) if you meta-lawyer your way into it or even by accident. Tons of discoveries.

PROS:
- Game looks like the best eye-candy retros and the beloved VS

- Gameplay. Seriously it's just CRAZY fun. Buy it for you, your friends and family

- Classic dungeon crawling with a really cool twist. Quite a few of them actually.

- Unlockables - you unlock LITERALLY everything with hard work. If you're smart you do it quickly or you can mindless-grind if you want for some reason.

- Cool devs. Worth supporting. If VS is anything to take note of here, then this game has awesome future and more ahead of it.

- Will work on potato PC's and won't break (probably) like VS can with insane builds, unless you own a NASA PC.


CONS:
- Game looks a bit bad on larger screens with higher resolutions. The pixels are gargantuan. Easily fixable with just playing in a window or image scaling. Would cry about some interface scalin ingame though.

- Mechanics are a bit unclear. Some abilities and triggers for them are completely unexplained. Weapon cards do have damage on them... But they behave so wildly different and movements have so much randomness to them sometimes, that damage amouts don't really even count as suggestions. Also no enemy or even boss health bars.  The game is supposed to be a chaotic "wildcard" so I suspect this might be intended though. ^^' You're better of seeing what works for you, or even better just using what you like and looks cool. There is also a possibility you unlock info and I just haven't done it yet. It's very likely here actually.

- The game is just released so it's bound to have some bugs. I haven't noticed any so far, except some minor glitches when you rotate in the village too much and too fast. Actually overall the level of polish could be a PRO, considering what state are many "1.0" releases nowerdays.


Overall: BUY THE GAME. YOU WILL NOT REGRET IT. If there was a scale in the recommend menu it would get a 10/10 even with those minor troubles.

Overall, I'd recommend this but keep in mind it's a survivors-like first and a card game second.

The main strategy of the gameplay revolves around the combo system, where you play cards of ascending cost (0 -> 1 -> 2 etc) to increase their effects, with some cards (Wild cards) allowing you to start back at 0 cost cards while maintaining the combo. This effect stacking can make you very strong very quickly. This keeps the gameplay interesting early on.

The progression in the game is as enjoyable as the original VS, as the upgrades make you feel more powerful very quickly. All the classic VS upgrades are back but turned into card game form, including weapon evolutions during gameplay. The characters (crawlers) themselves also come with effects that'll change how you want to deck build to a certain extent.

But once you are around the midgame, the game really just becomes a card spam simulator. While this is effectively how the power fantasy of Vampire Survivors translates into a card game, it gets old quickly as you just spam the "Play All Cards" button and watch yourself draw your entire deck multiple times. There *is* a mechanic that punishes you for it, but by the late game it's effectively a joke and you could actually end up using it to just farm more currency.

The only challenging part was defeating the Red Reaper that kills you at the end of runs, but even that's not hard at all assuming you build your deck with that goal in mind. I *know* it's not supposed to be difficult - as it's a survivors game at its core - but challenging content is really going to be needed in the future to keep this replayable.

There is nothing else to do beyond that. You can farm gold to "upgrade" gem rarity - gems being upgrades you can add to your cards during a run - by making rarer ones appear more often, but I didn't even touch that mechanic before beating the entire game, and you might as well ignore it considering how much more expensive it is compared to every other upgrade.

I finished the entire game in 13 hours. I did enjoy my experience with it and would recommend it at its cheap price, but if you are looking for a time sink survivors game I would wait until this gets more content, and if you are looking for a card game with strategy elements this really isn't it.

good god someone has to stop them. if poncle keeps making games like this, i won't have time to do anything else besides play them. VS was an instant hit for me, and so is VC. probably some of my favorite games of all time.

Edit: This was crack cocaine until I crashed. I'm a bit surprised at how quickly I completed most objectives, and specifically, cleared all levels. Hopefully there will be many reasons to come back to this, and I could see myself doing another hand once in a while. What I will say though is that every hour I did spend with this game was truly incredible. Masterclass in addictive and satisfying gamplay!

The combo system is quite something, very fun to play with and it creates natural flows in different decks you build that feel unique from each other in many cases. I enjoyed seeing how all the old weapons were adapted here, and I thought it was cool that the evolutions remained the same so a few of them simply came to my mind naturally as goals to chase, cards to watch for... 

The bosses near the end become way too spongy, though. The last level or two have boss fights that seem to take forever, and you basically have to have some kind of cracked build to avoid what is normally relentless damage. The final boss was super super cool, totally worth getting through those last levels, but it does make me wonder how more challenge could be added in future updates without risking boredom.

More characters, more cards, and different levels would all be worth a revisit. I would really like to be able to click the map and go to a location (as much as I enjoyed the walking at first, it's quite brutal when you have to go back to a chicken meal) but that's my only serious gripe I think. The music was also fine but I prefered DJ sets soon enough. Still, gameplay-wise, this hooked me like almost nothing has is recent memory until I was sort of deposited, like from some hard drug, back into the sober realization that I'm playing a game, not doing the most satisfying loop ever invented. I miss those hours but I'm grateful for them.

I guess my final note would be that the arcana cards seem strange to me because one of them allows you to have nearly infinite mana by your second or third turn every fight, and/or, run a super powerful deck that requires tonnes of mana without constantly failing to play most of your cards... and then the other arcana cards do things that are far, far less useful than that (like reorder your deck or double the rare healing you receive). It feels like a "there's only one right choice here" situation for almost every character in the game, I don't really see the point of the others. A couple of them are nice to find as a bonus in a run but none compare to extra mana in a game like this. I was glad to unlock it and it's great to have I just sort of found all the others disappointing when I unlocked them. The unlocks in this game come at a great pace though, basically perfect, and there is a good gold sink for customizing your experience and strategizing for future combat with the blacksmith. Really well-balanced in every aspect except these arcana cards.

All in all, worth double the price, not even sale stuff just buy it. It's fun AF. Here's to more from these fine devs!

As someone who heavily struggles with StS and StS2, this game has made me appreciate what challenging mechanics brings to the table for longevity, satisfaction and enjoyment. It becomes far too easy too quickly, to the point you can essentially ignore most of the game mechanics and no longer have to really think and plan out your plays. Which is at odds with what the "deckbuilder" audience expects. 

Conceptually, the game is amazing. However it is in dire need of some balance changes to have any kind of longevity and challenge. Wild seals and wild cards are far too common for how they function. Though even if made more rare, you only need 1 wild to infinitely combo and win every fight turn 1, so I think there needs to be a mechanical change with how they work or how combos work. 

In its current state, I don't see myself being able to achievement hunt, let alone play beyond that. Though I imagine I could use seals to remove the problematic aspects and move around rarities a bit to create a more interesting run, I hope the devs create a mode that is more restrictive and actually lets this game's amazing concepts and mechanics shine in a more strategy-required format.

After having obtained 100% of the achievements in under 25 hours (including 7 hours from the demo), here are my two cents:

- Very addictive gameplay, yet also makes you think a lot despite and thanks to the chain and enemies.

- You can most definitely break the game if you wish - as long as you're careful not to anger the reapers

- Starts off rather slowly, but once it picks up, you're hooked.

- Forced to pick up a card at your level-up is a very interesting choice to muddle with your combo, and I'm in for it.

- That being said, rerolls are very important for you to get certain gems you are looking for. Too bad there aren't good guaranteed ways to get them other than the 10 at power-ups.

- Crawlers' abilities and starting decks can be important for many builds. I'm just a little surprised how little there are of them right now. Of course, that will be fixed with further updates.

- It took about 23 hours for me to get all the unlocks. A little short compared to VS, but there are definitely still WAY more to come, as it's confirmed post-release content will be made like in VS.

- Soundtrack is remixed songs from VS, and still VERY good. Filippo and Daniela do it again! And also shoutout the new composer, Michael Coviello, as well as the main menu theme guest composer, Yoko *FREAKING* Shimomura.

Overall, this is definitely as good - if not better - than VS in quality and gameplay. I can't wait to see what the devs have cooked up for further updates! This is a high recommendation from me, but if you feel like 30 hours of gameplay is not enough, feel free to wait a little for further updates. But the foundation is fantastic.

Poncle (and his team) are truly masters of subverting and exceeding expectations.

Vampire Survivors constantly surprised with more and more and more content that was increasingly absurd and broken; without spoiling anything, this game (so far) seems to do precisely that while being a card game. Contemporaries like Slay The Spire might allow the player to break the game a little, but Vampire Crawlers manages to still be a real challenge at times while not only allowing the player to break the game orders of magnitude more than Slay The Spire but even encouraging the player to do so.

Vampire Survivors popularized and perfected an entire genre, debatably creating it even. It remains to be seen, but I could easily foresee Vampire Crawlers doing the same. Is it a roguelike deckbuilder, which isn't anything new? Yes, however the craftsmanship with which it is built, in every regard, in addition to the iconic "break the game" mechanics that Vampire Survivors brought will cement this game (and poncle) in gaming pop-culture for years to come.

Astounding job devs!

The way the combo system works, it can create a deep (and deeply satisfying) flow state. Every time I extend a combo past the "natural" limits, little dopamine explosions go off in my brain. If you've played Vampire Survivors, you know the feeling, but you might not have expected a card game to achieve the same effect. It does.

[h1] Poncle Tries Something New & Crushes It [/h1]


[h2] I was very excited for this release, and I am delighted to say that I was not disappointed. [/h2]

The game is fun. Period. Just like in Survivors, the implementation of the roguelite elements are satisfying. But there is much more that Crawlers offers. 

With Survivors, once I had scaled and upgraded enough, I found myself bored as I simply went through each level going between choosing 1 of 3 and walking around avoiding the bad guys. 

This format is so much more interactive and luck-based.  The simple, foundational act of playing combos and attacking and stacking buffs is so instantly enjoyable. Broken and infinite builds feel that much more earned. 

With a quick gameplay loop and short runs, it is so easy just to go again and again and again. 

[h1] ★★★★★★★★★☆ [/h1]

Would recommend to anyone even marginally interested in roguelikes.

Vampire Crawlers (VC) is a fairly mid card game which has great ideas that's being pulled in two different directions to its own disparagement. 

On one hand the dungeon crawling starts off small and condensed and fleshes out into something mechanically bigger with interesting nodes on the dungeon map, that in the earlier parts of the game have actual fun choices for deckbuilding. 

This is quickly outpaced by the breakable nature of the cards themselves with a handful of Crawler cards being a notable fracture in the gameplay loop (Pugnala, Poppea). This pair alone I found myself using as my 2nd/3rd in every map after unlocking more starting crawler slots and quickly devolved into a degenerate gameplay loop: 

First combat of each run - Activate crawler cards - Find spellbinder, Attractorb, reusable Wild card/gem, Mana books - With a few unlocked rerolls this took on average the first dungeon floor completion to put together.
You can now reach 30x combo every turn as Pugnala will constantly cycle your deck playing the many yellow cards, and with Attractorb to completely refill your entire hand, while Poppea sustains your early duration while also providing a reusable wild card in the form of Song of Mana. 

The card combat quickly breaks down and gets more annoying instead of relaxing while still being mindless like the late game stages of Vampire Survivors (VS) when you have accumulated a stockpile of golden eggs. Unlike VS however, VC's problems are inherent to the integral gameplay loop and while you can disable upgrades via refunding the issues still persist even with only a single guaranteed crawler. 

I really do enjoy the combo system and the mechanics of the combat, the combo scaling, card effects and mechanics etc, but has leaned too far and became too easily exploitable, and while it was fun the first couple of times it quickly became extremely repetitive and boring having the option to play 100's of cards every turn. 

Gold as a resource is FAR too easy to obtain absolutely ludicrous amounts of thanks to both the overkill system and how Greed is stacked. Unlike combat stats, secondary stats like Growth (XP), Greed (Inc. Gold), and Luck all *permanently* stick around for the run when gained from card buffs like the Greed gem. Thanks to how easy it is to reach extremely high combo this results in maxing out your buffs (1000% cap) fairly early into a dungeon if you focus on it. Paired with the overkill system which has you gaining gold for overkill damage on bosses at a diminishing rate the more you get up to a total of 5000, can quickly ramp up gold gains as with 500% greed (base 100% with upgrades) will net you 25k per boss. The largest gold amount I have had in a run was >300k which meant every card with Coin Count was also dealing >300k, even at >10k gold this is still ridiculous for one upgrade. 

The gem system is a great and somewhat different take on an upgrade system that ends up feeling almost completely detached from the gameplay for 90% of the gem choices. Things such as Coin Count, Copy, Wild, feel almost mandatory for a large portion of cards while other choices which should be considered end up falling flat like the +x/-x Mana cost manipulation gems which conceptually are great just never felt correct to use over literally anything else. 

While ultimately you are free to choose what strategy to play with, everything still inched towards infinite cycling after the first couple of stages which became boring. 

Overall, VC is a great concept which shows it strengths early in the gameplay loop but pushes too far too quickly, resulting in an ever increasing dissatisfactory gameplay loop the further in the game you go. I am however interested to see if and how they continue releasing content for this as it does have good potential if it is reigned in a bit and expanded on the more interesting parts.

After 20 hours, I've beaten the game and seen the credits, and you know what, it was a fun time! I'm not the biggest rougelike deckbuilder fan, but this was a fun iteration of it. I don't think it's as anywhere as good as the OG Vampire Survivors, but it's certainly worth the price.

The biggest divergence from the others in this genre is the combo system, in which you want to play cards of increasing cost for multipliers to the numbers on the cards. Then you get modifiers to cards, allowing you to break them. The card pool is all based off of Vampire Survivors things, and is fairly shallow, so eventually an extremely dominant strategy emerges regarding deckbuilding: Take card draw, take mana gain, take wild cards. Looping your attacks in between raising your combo to obscene levels was the only way I could win later stages, and it wasn't for not trying other strategies. All my decks generally ended up with a pile of the same "goodstuff". Arcana (modifiers to your deck) and crawlers (characters from Vampire Survivors who provided synergy with cards of certain classes) didn't transform the experience enough. Still, following that one strategy was very fun! It was still a very numbers-go-up, flashy, addicting experience.

I found it curious that the game aggressively prevented infinite loops. Bosses have this mechanic where they do things after every X cards you play, so this wasn't really a problem, but I suppose it was reasonable to prevent them for non-bosses. Still, the Vampire Survivors brand is one of complete maximalism, so I was shocked to see restraint going on here. Maybe too much restraint. The game also touts itself as fast paced and furious; hitting the "autoplay all cards" button is a great way to lose a run, so that's a lie. You have to play your combos correctly to survive, and some animations pause the whole "you can play cards as fast as you can click" feature, so that's also a bit of a lie.

In general, this 1.0 release feels... Incomplete. Some crucial UX is missing, like hand sorting and full deck viewing in combat. Some stages are inexplicably missing variants. The bridge stages are all the same and that's boring. In general, the game needs more still. After 20 hours, I haven't come close to getting enough gold to max out everything; in Vampire Survivors, maxing out everything is the beginning, but here I feel like I've hit a brick wall of content. I'd like to see major changes to arcana or crawlers to actually make them change how you play the game and enable focused builds other than combometer goodstuff. But most importantly, there needs to be some more quality of life and more stages. I'd especially like an endgame stage that's infinite in depths, for money farming and challenge purposes.

Despite all that, I became quite addicted to this game and had a lot of fun crawling in it. I think it's a pretty nice 20 hours, overall. Fans of Vampire Survivors will like, but probably not love, this game. I think it could be truly great should it fix its holes in content and try to capture the feel of the original game more in its gameplay. I doubt they'll change the game in such major ways, but considering how often they add content to Vampire Survivors, who knows? I'm really hoping for updates for this one.

A solid spin-off that earns its own place
Vampire Crawlers takes the core appeal of Vampire Survivors and translates it surprisingly well into a turn-based deckbuilder. The combo system feels good once it clicks — playing cards in ascending mana order and watching the multipliers stack up is satisfying without being overwhelming.
The dungeon exploration keeps things interesting between fights, and the legendary weapon evolutions give you something to build toward each run. It respects your time whether you want to play methodically or just let the chaos take over.
Good value, especially with the bundle discount if you already own Survivors. Worth picking up.

I’ll be the first to admit that when Vampire Crawlers was announced, I was a skeptic. I had poured an embarrassing amount of time into Vampire Survivors, becoming completely hooked on its "bullet heaven" chaos. Because of that, I lacked the imagination to see how such a frantic, high-energy experience could ever make the jump to a card-based dungeon crawler. It felt like a recipe for a disjointed cash grab of a spin-off.

As it turns out, I couldn't have been more wrong. I should have known better... but these are cynical days and true beauty doesn't always reveal itself on first glance.

Vampire Crawlers is an absolute blast. It somehow manages to preserve the energy of its predecessor while transitioning into an entirely different genre. It feels like a legitimate successor to one of the most influential games of the last decade. If the original felt like lightning in a bottle (like a lucky formula that poncle just happened to discover) then Crawlers proves that this game developer isn't a one-trick pony. They clearly have a deep understanding of game design and a genuine respect for their audience.

A bloody ripper of a game for a BLOODY good time:
The Verdict: A Grade-A Bloody Good Time

Fair dinkum, I haven’t been this hooked on a new game in yonks, it’s an absolute cracker.

The Vibe: It nails that pixelated, chaotic charm of the original, but flipping it into a first-person dungeon crawler? That’s a proper galaxy-brain move. Feels fresh as, without losing what made it great in the first place.

The Gameplay: The combo system is downright brilliant. Lining up your cards just right and watching everything pop off in a glorious mess of carnage feels massive. When the screen lights up, it’s pure dopamine...no notes...and even when you get socked one...you're ready to come back for more in less than 10 seconds...no time wasting.

The Polish: Even this early on, it’s smoother than a cold tin on a scorching arvo. Card synergies click together beautifully, and the gameplay loop just flows. No mucking about. And you know more polish is coming in the inevitable dlcs this series is famous for.

If you’re into a good roguelike, do yourself a favour GET ROUND TO IT.

Why it’s a must-play:

Addictive Loops: Proper “just one more run”… ten runs later and you’re still going.
Smart Synergy: Building combos actually feels clever and rewarding, not just luck.
Top Value: Heaps of gameplay for bugger-all coin.

Safe to say this one’s gonna have me hooked for a long, long while yet.

Played for a bit and it's well made and fine as a distraction, but I'm not convinced that the numbers-go-up style of metaprogression fits here.

Couple that with the fact that there's no real decisions to be made during gameplay, as there's basically always a very obvious correct choice (and 90% of the time it doesn't even matter anyway if you go for it or not).

Feels more like you're watching a screen-saver than playing a game.

Which is fine for the genre that Vampire Survivors was in, but it's not what I want out of a card game.

I have 25 hours in the Demo. This game is still fun, but I'm not gonna lie, not a big fan of some of the changes in the game from the demo to release. The shields and some of the characters got massive nerfs, which make the game feel more artificially slow and frankly less rewarding. Vampire Survivors was a game that embraced the absurdly overpowered builds you could do with the right combos and enough understanding of the game, and I genuinely don't understand why it seems the devs are trying to move away from this with (the changes from the demo to full release of) Vampire Crawlers. It feels a bit like the devs don't quite understand their own community and what makes their games fun. I still love the studio and their games, Poncle is genuinely one of the best indie studios out there right now, but I hope this feedback finds them well and they at least reconsider their strategy here. Then again, this game just came out a day or two ago, maybe these are just growing pains. Either way it's still a fun game and I recommend buying it! 

UPDATE: After finishing the game, my critisism stands even firmer. The stat caps, odd limitations on gems such as Triple Damage gem not working on  Pentagram with no indication as to why and other little things like this make the game feel artificially slow. It is a very short game, there is no hiding it. But I wish the devs went more the route of adding replayability rather than adding artificial restrictions to slow down the player. It is just annoying and frustrating. 
To be honest, this game was a bit of a letdown. It feels like a random game wearing the skin of Vampire Survivors. I don't know if the team has changed between the games but this does not feel like a Vampire Survivors game. There is less content and replayability than in the first Vampire Surivors game, and honestly this doesn't feel worth the extra price at all. If hiring more employees and raising prices means worse product, that seems like a bad decision to make. Anyway, I'm still slightly hopeful for Warhammer Survivors, but pessimism is creeping in my mind. Please let the other game be more fun. Let me preface this by saying that I respect this developer, I *love* Vampire Survivors, and was super excited for this game -- but I am very disappointed.

The game itself is a great idea, but lacks any real diversity, dopamine, or feel good aspects.  You'll find yourself just blazing through it without a thought in the world, lazily pressing play all.  The assets are clearly from Vampire Survivors, but it doesn't do the game any justice.  The music, the look and overall feel of the game is a bit depressing and dreary, and never picks up pace.

I played through several rounds/levels of the game pretty seamlessly, but nothing felt different or special about each run.  It just felt like throw a card simulator, walk to the next monster, throw a card simulator, rinse repeat until you beat the level. 

What felt good in combining your abilities in Vampire Survivors, again falls flat here.  Nothing feels special.. just "ok" and you keep going.  Yeah it's cool to blow up a wall of minions... but you just have to go do it again, and again until you beat the level.  Once you beat the level and move onto the next... it feels exactly the same.  It gets entirely repetitive and boring, with no mind or skill needed to play the game.

I'm honestly sad and disappointed this wasn't better, but this developer tends to surprise us so hopefully if there are any future updates, it would be to add some sort of wow factor, or wonder to the game where it isn't just dungeon card simulator.

Good game so far! This is my first card battler really so I cant compare in that respect but here are my thoughts up to this point, with my negative or at least middling interactions being given more detail for hopefully constructive criticism.

Goods
Pacing is good, fast, difficulty is present but not too rough right now (pushing Tower rn)
loops are long but not insane in length.
Meta upgrades are abundant and keep me locked on as goals and reasons to play. 
Variety is good in weapons and upgrades but we could always use more.


The Not-So-Goods
1. leveling up can be detrimental and it can change your hand order severely. Not really sure how that would be mitigated, unless there is a 'hand-lock' power up I have not gotten yet. Organization in general could be nice, being able to see cards lined up by mana cost would be nice, or a small strategic X reacts to X type menu would be fun. 
2. A better tutorial for showing how bosses work. Didnt know till about 10 hours in monsters could guard attacks. More than that, it was not stated what the (what I am calling) Casting State is, where the boss will stop advancing, its outline changes and every card used against it counts down to its attack getting shorter every couple turns. Seems to be based off of HP but idk how much id assume its a 'phase 2'. 
3. Unless there is a character or mechanic I havent reached yet I'd like an Overcharge gem, or mechanic, or item, that dumps left over mana into damage (or armor). 
4. Following that, armor needs more options and needs to be expanded on as a mechanic. Its just barely useful as is. The Arcana that allows for armor damage needs so much more. 2x or 3x that damage and we'd START to cook with gas. At the end I'd like to see it as a secondary resource for certain characters.
5. I don't think the characters are unique enough (this may be a skill issue on my part in all honesty) most characters are some variation of X reacts to Y card color trigger. Just as in VS Id assume more further on characters will get more intricate but im not the biggest fan of what we got right now. 
6. Hand draws can sometimes lag behind and I've ended turn more than once just to see a useful card being drawn a second after by a chained orb card. Most likely this is a bug so take that as it may be.

Final note I'd love to see secrets just as in VS, stuff not on the map so on so forth. Invisible walls, weird missing no enemies, maybe with a less formed roguelike variant of maps with more variation. 

This game has great bones all together. If you can't pick it up now, keep an eye on it as itll only get better.  Even with all that above it is fantastic and a good way to burn an hour or so here and there just like VS.

if wondering if the game is good for you, here's the honest take.

It's a very new player friendly card game roguelite experience. If you have already played card game roguelite's AND you are looking for something similar, this is NOT the game for you. Still, it's a good game for what it is and deserving of the positive reviews it has, despite most of them being vampire survivor/poncle fans more than vampire crawler fans.

Difficulty is not really there. Because, this game takes the logic of Vampire Survivors to encourage the player becoming a literal immortal god. That same concept carries over here exactly. Imagine slay the spire with unceasing top and skecko eye, now imagine being able to start the game that way.

This game is fun for new players of cardgame roguelite genre and the best relatable example I can imagine is...
Final Fantasy Mystic Quest is the introductory game to RPG games as this game here is to cardgame roguelite's.

With that explained,
IF you have friends you want to get hooked on cardgame roguelite's such as slay the spire or any of the kind, THIS GAME right here is DEFINITELY how you can get them hooked on the genre before you send them into other similar games.
If you are also someone who finds many roguelite card games too hard or are just looking for a casual laid back chill experience involving cards, this game is worth full price.
The target audience is crystal clear and Poncle delivered the exact product those people want.


Extra note + note to the dev, relating to the trust of the game and the dev in a public statement:

Many of my reviews usually come from background understanding and opinions on how developers treat their community and how their games represent them in the matter of "Trust".
Poncle to this day has proven to be a "developer for gamers" and that's extremely rare in the industry even among solo devs that are out there. I am not afraid to call out BS and from what i've observed throughout many many years as a gamer and someone who understands the gaming industry very well, I'm amazed the developer soul for gamers and your primary audience remains unchanged. We need more developers who make games for gamers rather than developers who make games for money alone.
Stay true.

Vampire Survivors had this great loop of exploration, combination, reward and application that all exploded into a game that feels almost infinite.
And while repetitive, it never felt like you were doing the same thing twice, somehow. It was and continues to be a marvel of a game that created a new genre in which it still remains at the absolute peak.

Vampire Crawlers doubles that and gives it to the next person.
The immediate cascading breadth of this game has stolen the oxygen directly from my lungs, and at every turn, before, during, and after any given run, does it again.
There's just so much video game so quickly, it's unreal, lightning does in fact strike twice, and Poncle simply gets to have 2 bottles of lightning, I mean, why not?

As a deckbuilder, Crawlers snowballs quickly and purposefully, it puts infinity within your grasp almost always, while making you fear the day infinity won't be enough.
I'm excited to find out what truly reprehensible war crimes this game will have me commit on it's behalf and what pots of gold I'll find at the end of those rainbows.

While a lot of knowledge from Survivors carries over into Crawlers, it isn't required to start playing the game, experimenting and putting 2 and 2 together yourself is a solid half of the fun of these games, so do not fear going in blind.

Any price you pay for this game will be too little, I promise you.

Pretty much exactly what you'd expect if you've played Vampire Survivors - transpose literally everything from the original release into the format of a dungeon crawler with card based combat, with no real changes otherwise. The locations are the same, just dungeons now, and the characters and evolutions are all here, they're just expressed differently. The card system works by encouraging you to play cards in sequential order to stack their multipliers up, and you get new cards or add on jewels with every level. You'll eventually find out that the Attractorb cards (which let you draw), the Tome cards (which give you mana), and the various wild cards (which are free to play and let you wrap your multipliers around from higher cost cards to the lower cost cards again) are the ultimate meta, as this combination lets you infinitely stack multipliers, which lets you gain unlimited mana, which lets you draw your entire deck over and over again (including the wild and mana cards you already used that turn). Fairly simple, and there's no real surprises if you played Survivors, but decently fun, and I'm sure there'll be more added eventually.

I enjoy maze games as much as I enjoy rogue-style games, so I really enjoy this game. I did not expect to enjoy the card play aspect because I usually don't like card-battle video games, but poncle's version doesn't feel like a card battle. I think it's because the other side is typically not playing cards against you.

How it's similar to Vampire Survivors: same characters, same weapons, similar references, the same "create chaos" vibe, similar music, same sense of humor. Gameplay is a mix of strategy and luck, although you get to choose how much strategy you want to use. Gameplay sessions are about 30-40 minutes per map.

How it's different: Gameplay speed depends on how fast you want to move through a maze and how quickly you want to play cards. So, the game starts more slower-paced but builds speed as you go along. Gameplay involves collecting cards that have different effects, picking which ones to keep and which to discard for different effects, and collecting gems to add perks to different cards. There is a small learning curve to learning the right strategy for playing, collecting, and discarding your cards. There's also strategy in choosing which perks you want to add to which cards, and you'll have a few moments where you think "whoops, I shouldn't have done that." Luckily - or unluckily - you only keep your cards for one game round.

If you like maze games, card games, chaos, or Italian food references, you will enjoy this game.

it's a fun game, and feels like a creative and fun sequel to a historic game... but... 

something in the balancing feels off. It does sort of have the same feeling that you get in Vampire Survivors when your build gets out of whack and gets way too OP and you obliterate everything, but instead of it just being on auto-battle auto-fire you have to do the maths to execute the build. Makes sense for a deck builder, for sure, but the mechanism of combos and mana cost and how you build your deck overall does feel strange-- maybe a bit unintuitive, or messy. maybe I'll give it 100 hours or so and see if I still feel the same.

Edit: well, I gave it a bit more time and I can definitely say that, definitively, it's a fun game, but it doesn't come close to Vampire Survivors nor Slay The Spire, or most other notable rogue-lites in the genre in terms of replay-ability. Once I found out about the Play All button and that I could basically just plow through the entire level spamming that button I lost engagement with the game. I'm still not going to change my review to negative because it is a decent game, I guess my disappointment is just that I was so hyped for it and am comparing it to the Legendary Vampire Survivors that I've spent 100's of hours on. Oh well, I hope everyone else enjoys it more than me and all my respect to the dev.

I guess I'm crazy, because I'm not really feeling this game. There is some strategy, but it doesn't really feel like there's much. 
tl;dr if you want something you can play where you turn your brain off and see big number and flashy animations, this is probably for you. If you're looking for something with a lot of depth and strategy, well, one of the best deckbuilders ever made just came out a few weeks ago.

Anyway, here are my thoughts after about 40 minutes:
- You can't skip adding cards to your deck.  You honestly can't make many choices in general. Choose from your 3 card drop, choose from a few modifiers (gems) for your cards. I guess choose to skip fights if you want..? I'm pretty sure everyone is going to clear every floor every time unless they've got a really specific goal though
- Your starting hand is 3 cards and you start with 3 mana and mostly 0 drops. So you play your whole hand every turn. Maybe there's a lot of synergy if you keep playing, but everything I saw was very straightforward. Do damage, add armor (block), add mana, draw a card, get a little free XP.
- In typical Vampires style, you do not get to choose your target. So even if there are debuffs available eventually, you cannot choose to put them on the biggest threat. I imagine that means, if debuffs exist, they'll just be global "enemies do X% less damage" and that's about it. Which is no different than giving you more armor.
- As you get into the run, you do get card draw and more mana and things get a little more interesting trying to factor in the order you play the cards, but it doesn't seem like it'll be that great. Maybe I'm giving up too early, but I was legit starting to fall asleep while playing, and I'm not trying to miss the return window cuz I was napping.
- I know I only have ~40 minutes in the game, but every single fight was exactly the same. None of the enemies did anything other than attack or block, at least as far as I could tell. In the trailers and screenshots, I don't see anything to suggest otherwise. It seems like a game of out-damaging your opponents or dying.
- The game does not offer up a lot of information. I got a gem (modifier) for a card that gave me "+1 amount". Amount of what? What does that even mean? I haven't played Survivors for 3 years, so if it's knowledge I was supposed to carry over, you've got way too high of expectations for me lol
- Speaking of not a lot of information, I picked up a relic/powerup/item/whatever you want to call it that did something. Who knows what though, because I clicked continue too fast and didn't read it. So... now where do I view it? Nothing I click on or mouse over shows me the effect. I pressed every button on my keyboard and got nothing. Guess you just gotta hope for the best.
- Speaking of not a lot of information, who knew the starting character had a passive? Not me until I unlocked the second character and saw it in the character select building.

I'm coming off of 80 hours in Slay the Spire 2 and 12-ish years of playing Magic. You can definitely say this is a card battler, but in my opinion, it's not much of a deck builder.

Short Version: If you liked Vampire Survivors and are interested in dungeon crawling with deck building, buy it now.

Longer Version: Great game. I loved Vampire Survivors and this is a clever and fun way of diving back into that pixel covered world, but with a new and refreshing twist. This harkens back to the old dungeon crawling days of my Pentium II computer. The deck building, just like the items from the previous game, allow you to build some really OP decks. Once you figure out the formula for success all you need is a little luck and you'll start chewing through enemies.

Also, the music is just as amazing as you would expect from the series.

At $10 I'd say it is a pretty low risk investment. For those of us that stick around, I have no doubt this will get a lot of DLC add-ons just like Survivors did.

Poncle did it again. The same creator who made the massively successful Vampire Survivors has returned with a new perspective on the same world of Vampire Survivors with Vampire Crawlers. For a game that is only $9.99, this is a truly great game and an interesting new take on the original. Very fun and addicting. I have seen at least one complaint (from a ♥♥♥♥♥♥ PCGamer reviewer) about how the game requires no tactical thinking or strategy with the card play, but I don't completely agree with that. While playing in mana order is the naturally good strategy most of the time, there are many ways to play hands to your advantage. I strongly recommend this title and I'm very impressed with Poncle and his new game.

Cool looking game, I bought it because I really liked Vampire Survivors and was interested in how the devs would integrate deckbuilding in the Vampire Survivors world. 

Unfortunately not my thing at all. Gets repetetive very fast, only after a few hours of gameplay. What I miss from the gameplay is what makes deckbuilding what it is. There's no feeling of improvement in my desicion making or a better understanding of how cards interact with eachother. That's because the game presents basically no synergies apart from trying to ramp mana cost on cards. Also the difficulty feels very artificial in the way that if you lack enough upgrades you could lose a fight purely because of that. Which can be fine, but in conjunction with the lackluster depth of building your deck it just makes it annoying and boring.

TLDR: If you're a fan of deckbuilding games I would not recommend. BUT if you're sold just by the "juice" and old school vibes, go for it.

Vampire Crawlers is good. Lets get that out of the way first. If you liked Vampire Survivors you MAY like Vampire Crawlers. The shift to a dungeon crawler won't be for everyone, but my god is the game like crack cocaine for me as a fan of that genre. It starts out humble enough, you get your one crawler with nothing special. But about 4 hours in you learn that the game is is a snowball, and you're stuck in the middle of it. It gets exponentially more insane the longer you play. You go from playing okay 3 card combos to, entire turns that will make the most masturbatory Izzet Storm player blush (mtg reference for my homies in the crowd). 

The demo will do A LOT to show you exactly what you're getting into, and your save will carry over to the main game. If you have fun with the demo I would recommend getting the game outright. For 10 freedom bucks you will have so much more fun than other things on offer right now. 

I don't want to spoil everything. But as an idea, you'll go from 1 crawler and okay card combos to.... An entire 3 man squad, arcanas that give you extremely powerful build around passives, a smith that allows you to add gem slots to your known and unlocked cards, card EVOLUTIONS that are item mixes from Vampire Survivors (exact same combinations too), AND MORE. Tons to unlock, tons of dopamine, and most importantly! Tons of fun! Huge recommend from me.

When I first played Vampire Survivors, I didn't get it. The appeal wasn't there for me, the game felt too mindless, as you may hear many people say. At some point, though, I re-approached the game to give it another shot, and was immediately and fully hooked on the game. I think the game's main appeal is in the process of unlocking everything, and I think if you allow that to be your motivating factor, the game becomes more of a crafting-roguelike where your objective it to unlock as many of the achievements as possible.

This feeling is captured perfectly in Vampire Crawlers. Many describe it as a deckbuilding version of Vampire Survivors, but I'd personally describe it more specifically as a cross between Vampire Survivors and a traditional dungeon-crawler, with the use of cards as a means to represent what moves you can do, turn-based RPG-style.

That being said, there's some weird design decisions that I hope resolve themselves over time.

I think in regard to the game being a deckbuilder, it could use some work. The game highly incentivices mana-sequencing, but there seems to be no means to automatically sort cards. And in a game where the order that you play the cards is so incredibly important, not having a means to quickly sort them AND having a button that just plays all cards in the hand seems -- odd? I'm just not ever using that button. I think the intent is that your hands become so overpowered that optimization doesn't matter -- but at seven hours in, I'm not seeing that yet, if I mess up my mana-sequencing even a little I find that I'll easily die.

BUT, these qualms aren't meant to deter you from the game. Literally every possible issue I could've had with Vampire Survivors has been fixed, and I think I can trust Poncle to keep making games that I want to get day one, because even if some of the mechanics are a little odd or obfuscated for seemingly no reason, fundamentally, this game is extremely fun.

Also, there's a demo and your save transfers over, so if you're on the fence do that. But trust me, it's worth the asking price.

[h1]Slay the Balatro Crawler Survivors[/h1]
[b]Vampire Crawlers = Balatro + Slay The Spire + Dungeon Blobber presentation + Vampire Survivors Seasoning[/b]

What a weird way to get me to play Balatro again! I joke, but only halfway. The core mechanics of this game, the card combat, feels almost just like Balatro without using poker cards. You don't start with a particular deck, but instead choose a classic VS hero card to start with that you will want to build your deck around based on their abilities, somewhat like a Joker card that you actually have to play. Also somewhat akin to the Joker cards, you can choose a tarot card to pair with your hero that remains in constant effect. In Balatro, you work to create a hand to chain together huge combos, and in Vampire Crawlers you do the same by chaining cards with lower costs into cards with higher costs to get a multiplying effect. It is the same dopamine hijack common with these sorts of games that make the hours just zip on by as you watch number go up and hordes of enemies wiped in an instant.

The dungeon crawling element is very lightweight and only really evokes the general feeling of a tile-based "blobber" RPG. You aren't going to get lost, there are no puzzles or traps, the dungeon is more so about choosing the ideal order to take on encounters to maximize XP rewards and minimize risk. Along the way you will find new cards and upgrades that work a lot like stamps in Balatro. You will also sometimes be  able to upgrade your mana, evolve your weapons, or sacrifice cards for other cards, bonuses, or healing.

The part that is Vampire Survivors is mostly in the art direction and the very clever translation of the heroes, abilities, items, and enemies from an auto-shooter into a very different sort of game. It succeeds very well at this and the same 8/16-bit vibe is here along with the delightfully corny humor.

[i]Visit the typical Italian countryside if you...[/i]

[list]
    [*]🇮🇹Get turned on by the idea of mechanics that feel like a cross between Slay the Spire and Balatro
    [*]🇮🇹Asbolutely NEED more roast chicken lore
    [*]🇮🇹Want to play something that kinda feels like a dungeon crawler but without the maze navigation
[/list]

[i]Crawl away instead if you...[/i]
[list]

    [*]🙅Do want a dense, deep dungeon with exploration as a focus
    [*]🙅Don't like card-combat games, as this is at it's heart a card game and not really a dungeon crawler
    [*]🧄Are allergic to garlic
[/list]

The maestro of dopamine manipulation has done it again, but... I wouldn't say the game is perfect. Longer dungeons can feel very repetitious and a slog in much the same way playing VS can after spending 20+ minutes on a single level. It's grind-heavy as might be expected, but thankfull there are many things to unlock in the roguelite process. This game definitely doesn't reinvent any wheels, but it does put some sick spinner rims on them.

I’ll be the first to admit I wasn’t the biggest fan of Vampire Survivors, I bought into the initial hype and after playing it myself - I didn’t feel the same level of reward everyone else seemed to. Fast forward to more recent days, I’ve started to delve into the “deck builder” genre after dipping my toes into StS. That’s where my excitement for this game came from, and I’m happy to say despite its more basic approach, it’s as addictive as others made the original VS seem to be (I spent 4.5 hours my first time playing) take my opinion with a grain of salt, as I’m not a veteran or a highly skilled player of these sorts of games by any means. But this game is highly accessible, and while it doesn’t exactly hold your hand, it also isn’t on the same level of CBT of other games in the genre. It’s fun, has great pacing and sense of reward, and again is highly addictive. For the cost of admission, I’d say it’s 100% worth even if you (like myself) weren’t a fan of the OG.

It takes the soul of vampire survivors and turns it into a deck-builder. They artfully reuse assets such as weapons and power-ups as cards you play and the items found in maps enhance the way you play them. Map progression works similarly, both for new and existing maps, and you can expect your favourites to return - along with your favourite "crawlers" and weapon evolutions. The depth and replayability is here on day 1 with in-depth unlocks, achievements and enhancements to/for just about everything.

All-in-all, it's simply fantastic. I have no immediate memories of any company taking their own original IP and putting such a drastic spin on it to such incredible success.

Vampire Crawlers is basically Vampire Survivors but with its own twist. Instead of running around open maps while enemies swarm you from every direction in classic shoot-’em-up fashion, here you explore charming pixel-art dungeons that are procedurally generated and always feel a little different. You can also choose whether you want to fight every enemy you see or just deal with a few and keep moving.

Along the way, you find chests for upgrades, and leveling up gives you new cards or improvements for the ones you already have. The fusion system is also back, which adds even more depth and fun to the progression.

It’s incredibly addictive, was one of my most anticipated games, and it didn’t disappoint in the slightest. Everything feels great, everything sounds great, and the satisfying sound effects when you unleash waves of attacks on enemies are just fantastic. You can even try to defy death here, too.

All in all, it’s a fantastic spin-off and absolutely worth every cent.

It's not a bad game, it just doesn't do anything better than the million other games in the genre. My main issue though is that the game quickly becomes mindless. I've only put about 7 hours into it and in that time I've never failed a run, amassed more money than I need for upgrades, and the majority of that was just spamming the "play all" button. What kind of card game doesn't require you to think about what cards you're playing?? Theres even an in-built anti-infinites system, but you can circumvent this by just freezing the enemy on the turn it triggers, allowing you to continue spamming until you either win or die of boredom.

I uploaded a 60 second clip for anyone who wants to see what I'm talking about: https://streamable.com/8vpx95   (Despite how it looks, the video isn't sped up.)

For the first 3 or so hours of the game it actually feels like you're playing something, but eventually the cracks begin to show and before you know it you'll be spamming PLAY ALL too. There's probably people out there who enjoy this kind of thing, probably the same sort of people who enjoy staring at pretty lights.

(The game runs well, but for some reason it uncaps the framerate even with vsync enabled by default, but I fixed that by disabling and reenabling vsync manually.)

Vampire Crawlers is an absolute gem of a game that sneaks up on you in the best way possible. From the moment you start, it pulls you into its addictive loop of exploration, combat, and constant progression. The mechanics feel smooth and satisfying, and every run offers just enough variety to keep things fresh without ever feeling overwhelming.

What really stands out is how effortlessly the game captures your attention—before you know it, hours have passed as you chase that next upgrade or push just a little farther. It has that rare quality where it changes night into day without you even noticing, which speaks volumes about how engaging the experience truly is.

The visuals and atmosphere complement the gameplay perfectly, creating a dark yet oddly relaxing vibe. Combined with rewarding progression and a steady sense of achievement, Vampire Crawlers becomes incredibly hard to put down. If you enjoy games that are easy to pick up but tough to step away from, this one is absolutely worth your time.

Having completed the game 100%, unlocked all achievements, obtained all heroes (all of them, right?), and collected every possible upgrade and more, I can say that the game is incredibly addictive. I finished the final area using several different combinations of heroes and Arcana cards, and all of them turned out to be very powerful and had excellent synergy. The balance does break closer to the end of the game, starting around Gallo Tower, but I think that is exactly how it should be.

Overall, I would rate the game a 9/10. I took off 1 point for its length, since it can be completed in just 15–20 hours at a relaxed pace (I did have some saved progress carried over from the demo version), but I think future updates and DLC will fix that. For its price, the game delivers a huge amount of emotions, especially when you see familiar characters, enemies, and items, particularly if you have played Vampire Survivors.

This game truly makes my Vampires Crawl.

Essentially, Vampire Crawlers is a simplified take on a dungeon crawler genre mixed with a deck-building game and a roguelite, though gameplay-wise it's more similar to other deck-building games like Slay the Spire than to actual dungeon crawlers like Wizardry, Legends of Grimrock and such. 

You are not likely to find any deep dungeons filled with secret walls in this one, and neither will you have to draw a map just to stay on track without getting lost. What you WILL find, though, is amazing music, satisfying gameplay loop, a lot of unlockables, and absolutely NO vampires.

What makes it special, though, is how addicting it is. Vampire Crawlers is just as addicting as Vampire Survivors... And, more importantly, it's just as ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ fun. Pure, unadulterated, no-♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ fun. No deep story, no notes or environmental storytelling, no crazy mechanics that would make your head explode, nothing else but fun.

Try it. Your Vampires will Crawl too.
